addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
On Claude.ai shared conversation pages, there's no built-in export button. The easiest options are:

**Option 1 — Copy all text (quickest)**
1. Open the shared link in your browser
2. Press `Ctrl+A` to select all text on the page
3. Press `Ctrl+C` to copy
4. Paste it here with `Ctrl+V`

**Option 2 — Copy individual messages**
- Each message block usually has a **copy icon** (clipboard) when you hover over it. Copy them one by one.

**Option 3 — Save as PDF**
1. Open the shared link in your browser
2. Press `Ctrl+P` (Print)
3. Change the destination to "Save as PDF"
4. Save and send me the file (I can read PDFs)

**Option 4 — Browser "Save Page As"**
1. Right-click the page → "Save as"
2. Save as "Webpage, Complete" or "Webpage, HTML Only"
3. This saves the rendered HTML locally

The simplest approach is **Option 1** — select all, copy, paste here. If the conversations are long, you can do them one at a time and I'll process each one as you paste it.

The validation is almost certainly hardcoded for Chinese domestic number formats only -- it's checking the digit count and pattern, not just the dash. Chinese forms typically accept only:
Mobile: 11 digits starting with 1 (e.g., 13812345678) Landline: 3�4 digit area code starting with 0, dash, then 7�8 digits (e.g., 051212345678)
Your Brazilian number has the wrong length/pattern for both, so no formatting will satisfy it. Two ways around it:

Today's topic: Future, Conditional, and Obligation.

Three core structures for supplier meetings:

1. Simple Future vs. Going To

"We will send the quote by Friday" (decided, committed) "We**'re going to build** this in Italy" (plan, intention)

2. First Conditional (Real possibility) "If the payback is acceptable, we'll move forward" (likely to happen)

3. Second Conditional (Hypothetical) "If we had more time, we'd add load testing" (not likely, imaginary)

Quick check: Which one feels weakest to you? Or should I give you three example sentences -- one from each -- and you translate them naturally from a supplier-call perspective?

"It was really good to us to see what MazMac can do, and I'm looking forward to keep this conversation"  "It was really good for us to see what Masmec can do, and I'm looking forward to continuing this conversation." ("good to us"  "good for us"; "looking forward to keep"  "looking forward to continuing" -- after "looking forward to" use the -ing form.)
103	
104	The patterns worth drilling
105	If you fix just a few habits, your English jumps a level:
106	The biggest one is "depends of/off"  "depends on." You said it at least four times. It's always "depend on" in English.
107	Second, "looking forward to / looking for + verb." After "looking forward to," use the ing form ("looking forward to continuing"). For intentions, use "looking to + base verb" ("looking to produce").
108	Third, watch "any" vs "none" in negative sentences -- "none of them are automated," never "any of them are" when you mean zero.
109	And finally, "code"  "quote." That one's a vocabulary swap rather than grammar, but in a procurement meeting it matters: say "quote" or "quotation" for the price proposal.

All machines are manufactured in Italy (Stefano confirmed this explicitly when Franklim probed -- relevant because some of your other suppliers represent in Germany but build in China).  English correction and evaluation from technical meeting

Read what i said during the technical meeting with Masmec, my name is aluizio, so please correct my phrases, and avalie my english, suggest improvements

I'll read the meeting document first.

Viewed a file, ran a command
Here's an assessment of your English in the Masmec meeting, Aluizio, with corrections and improvements.

Overall assessment
Your English is solid and effective for a technical business meeting. You communicated complex concepts (modular test systems, electrical connections, conveyor logic, test timing) clearly enough that Stefano understood and confirmed each point. Your main strengths are vocabulary range and the ability to redirect and clarify when there was a misunderstanding (the fixture/drive automation point). The recurring issues are small grammar patterns: preposition choice ("depends of/off"  "depends on"), verb forms after "to" and "want/need" (using base verbs instead of "-ing" or infinitive correctly), and noun forms ("needings" isn't a word). None of these blocked communication, but fixing them will make you sound more polished.

Phrase-by-phrase corrections

1. "Just a point, Stefano, Franklim and Alissonn, they are developing the assembly line for this project."  "Just a quick point, Stefano. The patterns worth drilling
If you fix just a few habits, your English jumps a level:
The biggest one is "depends of/off"  "depends on." You said it at least four times. It's always "depend on" in English.
Second, "looking forward to / looking for + verb." After "looking forward to," use the ing form ("looking forward to continuing"). For intentions, use "looking to + base verb" ("looking to produce").
Third, watch "any" vs "none" in negative sentences -- "none of them are automated," never "any of them are" when you mean zero.
And finally, "code"  "quote." That one's a vocabulary swap rather than grammar, but in a procurement meeting it matters: say "quote" or "quotation" for the price proposal.

52	Advice for the broader process
53	Use the same script for everyone. Turn the questions above into a checklist and ask all five suppliers the same things. Build a simple evaluation matrix (technical capability, test-system experience, Brazil support, software competence, cost, lead time) and score each one right after the meeting while it's fresh.
54	Note the asymmetry in your supplier list. Comau and Mondragon are large machine builders; DMC is fundamentally a software/controls engineering firm; Autec and Develop are smaller integrators. They'll propose very different things. Decide internally whether you want a turnkey machine builder or whether you'd consider splitting mechanics and software. With DMC especially, ask who would build the mechanical side.
55	Listen more than you present. Spend maybe 20 minutes presenting the concept, then let them talk. The quality of their questions back to you is the single best indicator of competence. Write down what each supplier asks -- questions one supplier raises become things you should clarify in the spec for the others.
56	Protect the spec. If you haven't already, have an NDA in place before sharing the full document -- your spec reveals WEG production strategy and product details.
57	Be ready for the questions they'll ask you. Have answers (or honest "to be defined") for: number of product variants and production volume per shift (this drives whether cycle times are feasible), whether the three modules are one purchase order or separable, the target installation date, whether the line layout/floor space is fixed, and details of the WAU electronic loads (model, interfaces) since that's customer-supplied.
58	Capture spec ambiguities they expose. A few I'd anticipate: the vision system scope is "to be detailed later" -- suppliers will want at least a rough definition to quote; the load test recipe (load profiles, duration, what 50 �C cabin control tolerance means) needs more definition; and clarify what "similar products" means dimensionally so fixtures aren't a moving target.
59	One last thing: since your document says silence equals full compliance, remind every supplier verbally of that clause and ask them to explicitly list deviations in their quotations -- it's the clause that will protect you most during the proposal comparison.
60	Good luck in the meeting -- if you'd like, after it happens I can help you turn your notes into a structured comparison matrix for the remaining suppliers.

Orchestrated meeting strategy with technical questions, commercial considerations, and multi-su...
Great timing for this -- and your spec is solid groundwork. Since this is the first of five supplier conversations, the most valuable thing you can do today is (a) gauge whether Comau is genuinely a fit, and (b) refine a standard set of questions you'll ask everyone, so you can compare suppliers fairly later. Here's how I'd approach it.
Goals for this first meeting
Keep in mind this is discovery, not negotiation. You want to learn three things: do they have real experience with electrical test systems (not just mechanical automation), can they support you locally in Brazil long-term, and how do they react to your spec -- do they ask smart questions or just say "yes, we can do everything"? A supplier who immediately agrees to everything without probing is a red flag.
Questions about their experience and capability
Have you built end-of-line test systems for power electronics or drives before? Can you show references or case studies? (Comau is strong in automotive body/assembly automation -- test benches for VFDs with hipot, load banks, and supervisory software is a different specialty. Push on this specifically.)

https://claude.ai/chat/1a1b4ce0-9d1e-4eb0-96ea-e7d1e1e506fb

1/12

 6/18/26, 12:54 PM

Modular all drives supplier meeting preparation - Claude

Who would develop the test software and supervisory system -- Comau directly, or a subcontractor/partner? Where is that team located?

Have you worked with hipot testing and the safety architecture around it (interlocks, discharge circuits, monitored enclosures per NR12/NR10)? Brazilian NRs are specific -- ask if they've delivered NR12-compliant equipment in Brazil before.

What's your experience integrating customer-supplied hardware? (Your spec requires WEG PLC500, WEG components and WAU-supplied electronic loads -- some integrators resist using hardware they don't control. Ask how they handle warranty boundaries when the customer supplies key components.)

Do you have engineering and field service presence in Brazil? Where? What's the typical response time for support? (Comau has a presence in Betim/MG historically -- confirm what still exists and whether it covers Santa Catarina.)

Questions about how they'd approach your project
Walk them through the concept and ask: what risks or weak points do you see in this spec? (Your own document invites the supplier to propose improvements -- a good supplier will have opinions about the side-door/tray transfer mechanism, the rear docking connections at 60 A / 575 V, and the heated cabin.)
How would they solve the automatic high-current connection (docking) reliably over thousands of cycles? Contact wear at 60 A is a classic failure point.
How do they propose handling the product movement into the test position -- gantry, shuttle, servo conveyor? (Your spec deliberately leaves this open.)
What's their typical project methodology and timeline for a system like this -- design freeze, FAT, SAT, ramp-up? Rough order of magnitude in months?
How do they handle source code delivery? Your spec requires it -- some integrators push back or deliver compiled-only. Get their position on record now.
Virtual commissioning / digital twin capability -- your spec asks for simulation or prototyping. Comau markets this heavily, so ask to see what it actually looks like in practice.

Commercial and practical questions
Ballpark budget range for a system of this nature (they won't commit, but their reaction tells you if you're in the same universe). Where would the system be manufactured and assembled -- Italy or Brazil? This affects cost, lead time, import taxes, FAT logistics, and spare parts. Warranty terms, spare parts availability locally, and training (your spec requires operational and maintenance training -- ask in what language and where). Are they open to a phased approach (e.g., functional module first, then hipot and load) if budget requires it?

Modular all drives supplier meeting preparation - Claude

Advice for the broader process
Use the same script for everyone. Turn the questions above into a checklist and ask all five suppliers the same things. Build a simple evaluation matrix (technical capability, test-system experience, Brazil support, software competence, cost, lead time) and score each one right after the meeting while it's fresh.
Note the asymmetry in your supplier list. Comau and Mondragon are large machine builders; DMC is fundamentally a software/controls engineering firm; Autec and Develop are smaller integrators. They'll propose very different things. Decide internally whether you want a turnkey machine builder or whether you'd consider splitting mechanics and software. With DMC especially, ask who would build the mechanical side.
Listen more than you present. Spend maybe 20 minutes presenting the concept, then let them talk. The quality of their questions back to you is the single best indicator of competence. Write down what each supplier asks -- questions one supplier raises become things you should clarify in the spec for the others.
Protect the spec. If you haven't already, have an NDA in place before sharing the full document -- your spec reveals WEG production strategy and product details.
Be ready for the questions they'll ask you. Have answers (or honest "to be defined") for: number of product variants and production volume per shift (this drives whether cycle times are feasible), whether the three modules are one purchase order or separable, the target installation date, whether the line layout/floor space is fixed, and details of the WAU electronic loads (model, interfaces) since that's customer-supplied.
Capture spec ambiguities they expose. A few I'd anticipate: the vision system scope is "to be detailed later" -- suppliers will want at least a rough definition to quote; the load test recipe (load profiles, duration, what 50 �C cabin control tolerance means) needs more definition; and clarify what "similar products" means dimensionally so fixtures aren't a moving target.
One last thing: since your document says silence equals full compliance, remind every supplier verbally of that clause and ask them to explicitly list deviations in their quotations -- it's the clause that will protect you most during the proposal comparison.
Good luck in the meeting -- if you'd like, after it happens I can help you turn your notes into a structured comparison matrix for the remaining suppliers.

Comau -- Company Briefing
Who they are
Comau is a globally recognized leader in industrial automation, founded in 1973 -- the name comes from "Consorzio Macchine Utensili." Headquartered in Turin (Grugliasco), Italy, the group has an international network of 8 innovation centers and 14 production facilities in 12 countries, with more than 4,000 employees. They were born as Fiat's in-house machinery supplier, which shaped their DNA: they are fundamentally an automotive body-shop and assembly automation house. ZoomInfo
Comau

Ownership -- important context
For 50 years Comau belonged to the Fiat/FCA group and later Stellantis. In July 2024, Stellantis and One Equity Partners (OEP), a middle-market private equity firm, announced that OEP would make a majority investment in Comau, a spinoff that was part of the strategic agreement set during the FCA�PSA merger that formed Stellantis. So you're now dealing with a PE-owned company actively trying to diversify beyond automotive and win new customers -- which can work in your favor commercially, but ask how the new ownership affects long-term support commitments. Stellantis

What they do
Their portfolio combines advanced robotics, vision technologies, AI, and digital platforms, including joining, assembly and machining solutions for traditional and electric vehicles, robotized manufacturing systems, industrial and collaborative robots, exoskeletons, AMRs, and end-to-end battery manufacturing and cell formation. Through Automha, a fully owned subsidiary, they also cover intralogistics and warehousing automation. They make their own industrial robot line and offer project management, consultancy, and a training Academy. Comau + 2
Their strongest reference areas are automotive body-in-white, powertrain/e-mobility assembly, and battery manufacturing -- note that battery cell formation lines actually involve electrical test/charge-discharge processes, which is the closest thing in their portfolio to your test-system needs.

Presence in Brazil -- relevant for you
They have a real, long-established Brazilian operation: Comau do Brasil, based in Betim, Minas Gerais, with entities covering engineering services (founded 1995) and machinery manufacturing. Recent local work: Comau designed and implemented a

Evaluated supplier candidates and constructed tiered ranking framework
I read your spreadsheet -- nice pipeline, by the way: ~24 companies across China, Japan, Europe, USA and Brazil, with Comau today (June 11), Autec and Develop on the 15th, Mondragon on the 16th, and DMC on the 17th. Here's my honest assessment, organized as a tier list.
First, the criteria I'm ranking against, based on your spec: (1) real experience with endof-line electrical test systems for power electronics (hipot, load, functional test -- not just assembly automation), (2) software/test-engineering depth with source code delivery, (3) ability to support you in Santa Catarina long-term (NR10/NR12, training in Portuguese, spare parts, field service), and (4) size/cost structure compatible with a 3module line. One disclaimer: this is a fit assessment from each company's public profile, not a judgment of quality -- the meetings will confirm or destroy these rankings.
Tier S -- Best theoretical fit, prioritize these meetings
Bosch (Manufacturing Solutions, Brazil) -- Bosch's special-purpose machinery division builds assembly and functional test equipment for its own plants and external customers, knows electronics manufacturing intimately, is deeply fluent in NR12, and has the global standardization DNA your spec asks for. Local presence in Brazil. If they're interested in a project this size, they're arguably your strongest profile. Risk: they can be selective, slow, and premium-priced.
HAHN Automation Group (Germany) -- assembly and functional test automation is literally their core business, including EOL testing of mechatronic and electronic products. Strong fit for the functional/load modules. Risk: support from Germany; ask about Americas footprint.
Mondragon Assembly (Spain, with Brazil unit) -- automated assembly and test lines for electronics and solar (their solar lines include electrical testing stations), modular machine philosophy, and an established Brazilian operation. The combination of test experience + local support puts them high. You meet them on the 16th -- good.
Tier A -- Strong, worth serious meetings
BBS Automation (Germany, now part of D�rr Group) -- well-regarded builder of flexible assembly and test systems, global footprint including Suzhou. Test stations are a real competence. Risk: post-acquisition integration into D�rr; no Brazil base.

may be small for them, and cost will reflect their overhead.

Masmec (Italy) -- Italian builder of assembly lines and EOL test benches for automotive mechatronics and e-motors. Of your European list, possibly the most testbench-native company. Risk: no Brazil presence; mid-size company far from you.

DMC (Chicago) -- different animal: an engineering services firm, not a machine builder. But they are excellent at exactly what most integrators are weakest at -- test & measurement software, PLC/LabVIEW/TestStand, MES integration, traceability databases. Keep them in mind even if they don't win the whole scope: a DMC + mechanical-builder pairing, or DMC as software auditor, is a legitimate strategy. Ask in your meeting on the 17th who they'd partner with for the mechanics.

Autec (Joinville) -- I can't fully judge their technical depth in hipot/load testing, but being in your own city is an enormous practical advantage for commissioning, support, spare parts, training, and lifetime cost. If their test-system competence checks out in Monday's meeting, they jump to Tier S. Probe hard on: largest test system delivered, software team size, high-voltage experience.

PIA Automation (Suzhou) -- PIA is an international group (Austria/Germany/China) doing assembly and test automation including e-mobility EOL test. Technically credible. Risk: support chain from China/Europe to Brazil.

Tier B -- Plausible, but with significant gaps
Comau / FFT Produktionssysteme -- both are automotive body-shop and assembly giants. World-class mechanically, but EOL electrical test for power electronics is not their natural habitat, and their cost structures are built for automotive programs. Useful benchmarks; I'd be surprised if either is the best-value winner.
JR Automation (Hitachi) / Eclipse Automation (Accenture) -- big, competent North American integrators with broad portfolios including some test. Similar concern: generalists, premium overhead, no Brazil support base.
Harmontronics (China) -- large listed Chinese intelligent-manufacturing company, strong in battery/3C assembly automation. Capability likely exists, but remote support, documentation language, and NR12 compliance are real hurdles.
Aumaq, SPI, MCK (Brazil) -- Brazilian machine builders/integrators. Local support is a plus and pricing may be attractive, but verify whether they've ever built anything at this complexity level (automated docking at 60 A / 575 V, hipot safety architecture, supervisory + MES integration, heated cabin). For some of them this could be a careerdefining stretch project -- which can mean great dedication or great risk.

Three strategic observations
Your funnel is heavy on assembly-automation companies and light on test-system specialists. Companies like Comau, FFT, JR, Eclipse, Acieta build things that move and join parts; your project is mostly about things that energize and measure products. If you can still add names, look for dedicated EOL test bench builders for power electronics/appliances -- that profile (common in Germany, Italy, and the autoelectronics supply chain) matches your scope more naturally than body-shop giants.
Define your support requirement early, and let it kill candidates fast. Decide internally what's the maximum acceptable response time for a line-down situation. If the answer is "48h on site," half this list eliminates itself, and you save weeks of meetings.
Use the strong locals as your anchor. A realistic winning outcome for you might be: a Tier S international (Bosch, HAHN, Mondragon) for credibility and test know-how, benchmarked against Autec or another capable Brazilian for cost and proximity --

**Project name:** Modular All Drives Test System

**Purpose:** End-of-line (EOL) automated testing system for WEG variable frequency drives (VFDs/frequency inverters) production line.

**Installation site:** WEG facility in Jaraguá do Sul, Santa Catarina, Brazil.

**Deadline:** End of 2027 — commissioned in Brazil.

**Three test modules:**
1. **Hipot test** — dielectric/insulation test, max 4 kV, ~3–60 seconds per unit depending on drive
2. **Functional test** — validates current/voltage measurements (tolerance <10%), HMI operation via RS-485/Modbus (no HMI test needed per Julio), takes ~1.5–7 min
3. **Load test** — 50°C heated cabin (temperature only, no humidity control), ~6–20 min per unit; uses electronic loads up to 60A at 220/440/575V; fire suppression flagged by multiple suppliers as needed

**Architecture:**
- Modular conveyor system with free-flow pallet function (product pulled aside for test while line stays free)
- Automatic electrical connections (docking) — ~60A power connections; automatic connection only works if drive connectors are designed for automated mating
- One test position per module
- Parallelization: 1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load test modules
- Assembly line is a SEPARATE project (executed by Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)

**Software requirements:**
- Open-source languages ONLY: C#, Java, or Python — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery mandatory
- MES/traceability integration required
- PLC controls product via RS-485/Modbus directly

**Hardware requirements (customer-supplied):**
- WEG PLC500 (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- WEG portfolio components (drives, inverters, safety hardware)
- WAU electronic loads

**Key constraints:**
- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- NR10/NR12 Brazilian safety standards compliance
- FAT (Factory Acceptance Test) + SAT (Site Acceptance Test) required
- Global replication potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — project can be replicated globally (useful commercial argument with suppliers)
- "Silence = full compliance" clause in RFQ (suppliers must explicitly list deviations)

**Three quotes requested from each supplier:**
1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only
3. Full integrated line

**Why:** Modernize VFD production testing, achieve full automation, global replication capability.

**How to apply:** This is Aluizio's primary work project. All supplier evaluation, email drafting, meeting preparation, and technical documentation relates to this scope.

**How to apply:** Default to concise emails. Offer two versions (warm/direct) when tone is ambiguous. Include time zone guidance when scheduling with China.

---

## Portuguese writing (Brazilian)

- No travessões (dashes) — not standard in Brazilian Portuguese; use comma, colon, or rephrase
- Acentuação obrigatória — always use proper accents (ã, ç, é, etc.)
- Column/variable names in code must stay without accents (technical constraint)
- Natural, first-person voice in academic/personal writing
- Register for master's work: competent student, not beginner, not impersonal

**Why:** He explicitly corrected a version that removed accents and used too many dashes. Confirmed the rule is "only remove dashes."

---

## English

- C1 level — correct his errors but don't over-explain; he gets it quickly
- Key recurring errors: "depends of/off" → "depends on", "any" → "none" in negatives, "code" → "quote", "looking forward to + -ing", "enter in" → "enter" (no preposition)
- Hesitation/fillers (um, uh) — point out but don't dwell on it
- Technical vocabulary is strong; collocations and prepositions are the gap

---

- Prefers interactive HTML trackers / dashboards for management tools
- Likes clean downloadable artifacts (Word, Excel, Markdown, Jupyter notebooks)

content.content:
1	MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM
2	
3	1. OBJECTIVE
4	This document aims to present the characteristics and requirements for a new test system concept, the Modular All Drives, which consists of the development of a modular, compact and automated test platform, designed to separate each test stage into an independent module, ensuring all stages necessary for validation of products from the Orion line and similar WEG drives of mechanical sizes A, B and C.
5	The system encompasses automated electrical connections, drive hardware, control software, safety systems and an integrated conveyor system in a single solution, allowing greater standardization, operational flexibility, setup reduction and optimization of the production process.
6	Within this concept, the present scope aims at the manufacturing of three main test modules: a hipot test module, to ensure the electrical insulation of the power connections of the drives, a functional test module, responsible for the electrical, logical and operational validation of the products, and a load test module, intended for the simulation of real operating conditions through the application of controlled load on the tested drives.
7	NOTE: The devices represented in this document are for illustrative purposes only, serving for explanation of the concepts. All details of the final project must be discussed and
8	validated for the supply, as well as the supplier may (and should) propose improvements aiming at the technical enhancement of the solution.
9	2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS
10	The project refers to the supply of a hipot test module, a functional test module and an automated load test module intended for validation of the functionalities of products from the Orion line of mechanical sizes A, B and C, as well as similar products.
11	Hipot Test Module:
12	The hipot test module consists of a station dedicated to the execution of dielectric tests on products from the Orion line and similar products of mechanical sizes A, B and C, with the objective of validating the integrity of the electrical insulation and ensuring the conformity of the product with the electrical safety requirements established by the applicable technical specifications.
13	The proposed concept encompasses an independent, compact and modular module, integrated into the production line, allowing the execution of the hipot test in an automated, safe and standardized manner.
14	The system shall have its own conveyor, enabling the automatic transportation of the fixtures to the dedicated test position.
15	
16	WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner
17	
18	Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 1 of 9
19	
20	 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM
21	
22	Upon arriving at the station, the product shall be automatically transferred to the test area, where the coupling of the electrical connections necessary for the application of the test voltage shall occur. The module shall have all devices necessary for execution of the test, including the hipot tester, protection devices, automatic grounding, discharge circuits, safety monitoring and electrical and mechanical interlocks.
23	Due to the critical nature of the test, the module shall have a safety enclosure with monitored access, ensuring the safety of the operators during execution of the test. The system shall automatically prevent the opening of the test area during the application of high voltage, in addition to performing the safe discharge of the product at the end of the test cycle. The module shall have wiring with insulation level for up to 4 kV in order to avoid leakage and noise.
24	The module shall include integration with the supervisory system and test software, allowing automatic identification of the product through QR Code reading, loading of the corresponding recipe, recording of test results and traceability of process information.
25	
26	Functional Test Module:
27	The proposed concept consists of a compact, modular and scalable test module, developed to execute functional test routines on the products, ensuring that all functionalities of the drive are in accordance with the test specifications and that the product is considered approved for use.
28	The module shall have its own conveyor integrated into the structure of the equipment, responsible for the movement of the fixtures containing the products along the production line. The product shall automatically travel on the conveyor to the region of the test module, where it shall be removed from the line and transferred to a dedicated functional test position. This architecture shall allow keeping the main conveyor free for continuity of the production flow and future expansions of the line.
29	The system shall integrate in a single equipment all hardware necessary for drives, measurements, protections and execution of the tests, eliminating the need for additional external panels.
30	For automatic identification of the product under test, the module shall have a QR Code reading system, responsible for identifying the drive and automatically loading the corresponding test recipe. In addition, the module shall have an integrated vision system, allowing visualization of the HMI and other sensitive regions of the drive, enabling automatic inspections and visual validations that shall be detailed later according to the progress of the project.
31	
32	The tests to be executed are: � External energization of the control, validation of measurement and operation of the HMI; � Energization of the product through the power section, validation of link measurement,
33	generated control power supplies; � Firmware version verification;
34

file.content:
MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

1. OBJECTIVE
This document aims to present the characteristics and requirements for a new test system concept, the Modular All Drives, which consists of the development of a modular, compact and automated test platform, designed to separate each test stage into an independent module, ensuring all stages necessary for validation of products from the Orion line and similar WEG drives of mechanical sizes A, B and C.
The system encompasses automated electrical connections, drive hardware, control software, safety systems and an integrated conveyor system in a single solution, allowing greater standardization, operational flexibility, setup reduction and optimization of the production process.
Within this concept, the present scope aims at the manufacturing of three main test modules: a hipot test module, to ensure the electrical insulation of the power connections of the drives, a functional test module, responsible for the electrical, logical and operational validation of the products, and a load test module, intended for the simulation of real operating conditions through the application of controlled load on the tested drives.
NOTE: The devices represented in this document are for illustrative purposes only, serving for explanation of the concepts. All details of the final project must be discussed and
validated for the supply, as well as the supplier may (and should) propose improvements aiming at the technical enhancement of the solution.
2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS
The project refers to the supply of a hipot test module, a functional test module and an automated load test module intended for validation of the functionalities of products from the Orion line of mechanical sizes A, B and C, as well as similar products.
Hipot Test Module:
The hipot test module consists of a station dedicated to the execution of dielectric tests on products from the Orion line and similar products of mechanical sizes A, B and C, with the objective of validating the integrity of the electrical insulation and ensuring the conformity of the product with the electrical safety requirements established by the applicable technical specifications.
The proposed concept encompasses an independent, compact and modular module, integrated into the production line, allowing the execution of the hipot test in an automated, safe and standardized manner.
The system shall have its own conveyor, enabling the automatic transportation of the fixtures to the dedicated test position.

WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner

Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 1 of 9

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

Upon arriving at the station, the product shall be automatically transferred to the test area, where the coupling of the electrical connections necessary for the application of the test voltage shall occur. The module shall have all devices necessary for execution of the test, including the hipot tester, protection devices, automatic grounding, discharge circuits, safety monitoring and electrical and mechanical interlocks.
Due to the critical nature of the test, the module shall have a safety enclosure with monitored access, ensuring the safety of the operators during execution of the test. The system shall automatically prevent the opening of the test area during the application of high voltage, in addition to performing the safe discharge of the product at the end of the test cycle. The module shall have wiring with insulation level for up to 4 kV in order to avoid leakage and noise.
The module shall include integration with the supervisory system and test software, allowing automatic identification of the product through QR Code reading, loading of the corresponding recipe, recording of test results and traceability of process information.

Functional Test Module:
The proposed concept consists of a compact, modular and scalable test module, developed to execute functional test routines on the products, ensuring that all functionalities of the drive are in accordance with the test specifications and that the product is considered approved for use.
The module shall have its own conveyor integrated into the structure of the equipment, responsible for the movement of the fixtures containing the products along the production line. The product shall automatically travel on the conveyor to the region of the test module, where it shall be removed from the line and transferred to a dedicated functional test position. This architecture shall allow keeping the main conveyor free for continuity of the production flow and future expansions of the line.
The system shall integrate in a single equipment all hardware necessary for drives, measurements, protections and execution of the tests, eliminating the need for additional external panels.
For automatic identification of the product under test, the module shall have a QR Code reading system, responsible for identifying the drive and automatically loading the corresponding test recipe. In addition, the module shall have an integrated vision system, allowing visualization of the HMI and other sensitive regions of the drive, enabling automatic inspections and visual validations that shall be detailed later according to the progress of the project.

The tests to be executed are: � External energization of the control, validation of measurement and operation of the HMI; � Energization of the product through the power section, validation of link measurement,
generated control power supplies; � Firmware version verification;

WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner

Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 2 of 9

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

� I/Os; � Serial number programming; � MAC address programming; � Ethernet ports; � USB port; � Internal battery validation; � RS-485 communication; � Motor operation and output current measurement; � Validation of the safety stop module; � Rheostatic braking; � Test of ground fault and output short-circuit protections; � Programming of factory default parameters.

These tests shall be executed in an automatic sequential manner, controlled by a WEG PLC500, using a human-machine interface for visualization and control, which may be a computer or a touch screen.
On this screen there shall be monitoring of the tests in progress, as well as detailed display of the information of the test results, whether approval or rejection, tools for manual execution of the tests, monitoring of the drives and registration of new products into the system.
The module shall have its own supervisory software that allows integration with the supervisory software of the other modules. Each module shall have its own independent conveyor, allowing new modules to be added side by side in a simple manner, without the need for complex mechanical couplings between conveyors. The concept shall prioritize ease of replication, modularity and global standardization.
The products shall be assembled on dedicated fixtures, responsible for mechanical positioning, electrical connection and interface with the test system. These fixtures shall have connectors and harnesses previously connected to the product, and shall have, in some part of the fixture, electrical contact pads, connectors or similar elements that make the connection between the system and the fixture under test.
The system shall support currents up to 60 A and voltages of 220 V, 440 V and 575 V. The fixtures shall support products with mass up to 30 kg without deformations or loss of dimensional accuracy.

Load Test Module:
The load test module consists of a station dedicated to the execution of load tests on products from the Orion line and similar products of mechanical sizes A, B and C, with the objective of validating the functional behavior, electrical performance, taking the product to maximum stress with the heated cabin.

WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner

Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 3 of 9

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

The proposed concept encompasses an independent, compact, modular and scalable module, integrated into the production line and developed to automatically execute the load test routines according to the recipe defined for each product model.
The system shall allow the controlled application of load on the equipment under test, enabling validation of electrical parameters, operational response, operating stability, protections and other characteristics defined by the test specification.
The module shall have its own structure for automatic movement of the fixtures containing the products, and may operate integrated with the main conveyor of the line or through a dedicated transfer system. Upon arriving at the test position, the product shall be automatically coupled to the system of electrical connections and interfaces necessary for execution of the tests.
All infrastructure necessary for execution of the tests shall be integrated into the module, including power systems, drive devices, electrical protections, measurement systems, communication interfaces, wiring, ventilation and other components necessary for safe and continuous operation of the equipment, without the need for additional external panels.
The module shall use electronic loads as load for the drives, which shall be supplied by WAU. The system shall support currents up to 60 A and voltages of 220 V, 440 V and 575 V. It shall also have a heated cabin controllable up to 50�C.
The system shall have supervisory software and test software, allowing automatic identification of the product through QR Code reading, automatic loading of the test recipe, parameterization of the tests, real-time data acquisition, recording of results and traceability of process information. It shall also allow integration with the other supervisory software of the other modules.
In addition, the module shall be designed considering operational safety criteria, ease of maintenance, accessibility to internal components, adequate thermal dissipation and possibility of future expansion for new products, power ratings and test architectures, maintaining the modular and standardized concept of the Modular All Drives system.

General Characteristics for the Modules: � 380 Vac 60 Hz power supply; � The mechanical design of the modules shall be as compact as possible considering factory floor
space savings; � Fixture return conveyor at the lower part, where the operator or an elevator at the end of the
line will position the fixture on the conveyor that will return it to the beginning of the line; � Approximate cycle times: Hipot test < 1 min, Functional test < 5 min, Load test < 20 min, however
the times will depend on the product and the test routine employed; � Fixture made of resistant material and with lifting points or handling points; � Consider 5 types of fixtures with 3 units per type, totaling 15 fixtures, each fixture type shall be
made according to a type of product to be mechanically fixed;

WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner

Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 4 of 9

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM
� The largest product to be tested shall have H x D x W = 460 mm x 300 mm x 220 mm, the fixture shall accommodate the product and all wiring;

� All fixtures shall have standardized dimensions. The only variation among them shall be the fixture nest, which shall be adapted for the fixation of each type of product;
� Identification by QR code on the fixtures; � Mechanical Poka-Yoke for positioning the product on the fixture; � For the hardware, wherever possible, use WEG components, which shall be supplied by WAU; � Automatic system for movement and positioning of the product; � Products rejected in any of the modules shall return to the conveyor and follow the path to the
end of the line, however with the indication that they were rejected in the supervisory system database. This information shall be used later in a final conveyor that shall direct the approved product to packaging and the rejected product to a repair/analysis station; � The communication architecture shall be used according to the need of the module or product; � Computer vision system for optical inspection of the product; � Doors and/or transparent windows that allow full visualization of the product during the tests; � Automatic side doors for passage of the fixtures between modules; � Structure that allows easy access for mechanical, electrical and pneumatic maintenance; � Safety system according to NR12, IEC and WEG standards, including interlocks, emergency buttons and movable guards; � Integration with MES, traceability and test database; � Project optimized for high availability, simplified maintenance and ease of global replication; � Each module shall have only 1 test position; � Each module shall have its own test software and supervisory software, with the supervisory software capable of integration with the other modules.

WEG Drives & Controls � Automation Ltd. Drives Production Department � Industrial Engineering Section Prepared by: Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner

Revision 00 � may/2026 Page 5 of 9

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM
The supplier's scope shall include: � Complete mechanical design; � Complete electrical design; � Software and automation development with source code supply; � Manufacturing, assembly and installation; � FAT, SAT and startup; � Schedule with preventive maintenance dates; � Operational and maintenance training.
The concept shall prioritize constructive simplicity, robustness, operational safety, ergonomics, ease of expansion and global standardization of the solution. The way in which the drives shall be used for movement of the product to the test position shall be defined in detailed technical meetings with the continuation of the project.
3. ILLUSTRATIVE IMAGES OF THE MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

Illustrative imagem of the All Drives test module

 MODULAR ALL DRIVES TEST SYSTEM

Illustrative image of a complete test line with several Modular All Drives

1. Product is assembled on the fixture and connected; 2. Automatic QR Code reading by the assembly stage;
3. Automatic recipe selection by the system; 4. Automatic movement of the product to the hipot test module via conveyor;
5. Automatic movement to the test position; 6. Electrical/mechanical connection; 7. Test execution; 8. Data recording; 9. Approval/rejection; 10. Return to the conveyor;

input.content:
---
name: project-modular-all-drives
description: Aluizio's main work project — automated EOL test system for VFD (frequency inverter) production at WEG. Internal codename ORION.
metadata:
  type: project
---

**Project name:** Modular All Drives Test System
**Internal codename:** ORION (folder: 2026-04-ORION)
**Document author:** Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner, Revision 00, May/2026

**Purpose:** End-of-line (EOL) automated testing system for WEG variable frequency drives (VFDs/frequency inverters) production line — Orion line and similar drives, mechanical sizes A, B and C.

**Installation site:** WEG Drives & Controls — Automation Ltd., Drives Production Department, Jaraguá do Sul, Santa Catarina, Brazil.

**Deadline:** End of 2027 — commissioned in Brazil.

---

## Products covered

- **Line:** CFW series (Orion and similars) — mechanical sizes A, B, C
- **Naming convention:** CFWn00A/B/C followed by current and voltage code
- **Current range:** 2.2A to 60A+
- **Voltages:** 220V, 440V, 575V
- **Max product dimensions (fixture must accommodate):** H×D×W = 460mm × 300mm × 220mm
- **Max product weight:** 30 kg
- **Models include:** CFW900, CFW500/501, CFW11 and Orion variants

---

## System architecture (visual concept)

The system is a conveyor-based modular line. Each module is a self-contained enclosure (WEG-branded, tower light, polycarbonate windows) with:
- **Side door** that opens automatically when fixture arrives
- Fixture with product slides **into the rear** of the module
- **Automatic electrical connections** are made at the rear of the module (docking)
- Test is performed inside
- Fixture exits to the right, returns to conveyor
- Each module has its own return conveyor at the lower level

**Full line flow:**
Assembly → Hipot Test → Functional Test → Load Test (Heated) → Packaging

---

## Three test modules

### 1. Hipot Test Module (Tensão Aplicada)
- Dielectric test — validates electrical insulation of power connections
- Insulation level: **up to 4 kV** (wiring rated for 4kV to avoid leakage/noise)
- Cycle time: **< 1 minute**
- Safety enclosure with monitored access; automatic grounding; discharge circuits
- Automatic prevention of door opening during high voltage application
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading

### 2. Functional Test Module
- Complete electrical, logical and operational validation
- Cycle time: **< 5 minutes**
- Controlled by **WEG PLC500** (mandatory, non-negotiable)
- HMI: computer or touch screen for monitoring, results, manual test execution, new product registration
- Integrated **computer vision system** for HMI inspection and optical validation
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading
- **Test sequence (sequential, automatic):**
  1. External control energization; HMI measurement and operation validation
  2. Power section energization; link measurement, control power supplies validation
  3. Firmware version verification
  4. I/Os
  5. Serial number programming
  6. MAC address programming
  7. Ethernet ports
  8. USB port
  9. Internal battery validation
  10. RS-485 communication
  11. Motor operation and output current measurement
  12. Safety stop module validation
  13. Rheostatic braking
  14. Ground fault and output short-circuit protection tests
  15. Factory default parameter programming

### 3. Load Test Module (Heated)
- Stress test under controlled load and temperature
- Cycle time: **< 20 minutes**
- Heated cabin: **controllable up to 50°C** (temperature only — no humidity control)
- Electronic loads supplied by **WAU** (WEG group company)
- Supports currents up to **60A** and voltages **220V, 440V, 575V**
- Real-time data acquisition; recipe-driven

---

## Fixtures

- **5 types** × 3 units per type = **15 fixtures total**
- All fixtures have **standardized external dimensions** — only the mechanical nest varies per product type
- QR Code identification on each fixture
- Mechanical **Poka-Yoke** for product positioning
- Must support products up to 30 kg without deformation
- Pre-wired with connectors/harnesses connected to the product
- Fixture contact pads/connectors at rear for automatic docking connection
- Material: resistant, with lifting/handling points
- Fixture return conveyor on **lower level** of modules

---

## General module specifications

- Power supply: **380 Vac, 60 Hz**
- Structure: aluminum profile frame + **5mm polycarbonate** enclosure/doors
- Base: reinforced beams **100mm high** for manual pallet truck transport + flanges
- All cabin materials: **non-combustible** (or flame-retardant + non-toxic); manufacturer report required
- Structure prevents body access into cabin with doors closed
- **1 test position per module** (no parallel positions within one module)
- Parallelization is achieved by adding more modules (1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load modules)
- Automatic side doors for fixture passage between modules
- Computer vision system for optical inspection
- Transparent windows for full product visibility during tests
- Easy access for mechanical, electrical and pneumatic maintenance
- Fire suppression system (flagged by multiple suppliers as necessary — not yet formally in scope as of June 2026)

---

## Operational flow (26 steps)

1. Product assembled on fixture and connected
2. Automatic QR Code reading at assembly stage
3. Automatic recipe selection
4. Automatic movement to hipot module via conveyor
5. Automatic movement to test position
6. Electrical/mechanical connection
7. Test execution
8. Data recording
9. Approval/rejection
10. Return to conveyor
11–17. Functional test (same sequence as above)
18–24. Load test (same sequence)
25. Final segregation: approved → packaging; rejected → repair/analysis
26. Fixtures return via lower return conveyor

**Rejected products** stay on line with rejection flag in supervisory database — segregated at end of line.

---

## Software requirements

- **Languages: C#, Java, or Python ONLY** — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery **mandatory**
- Each module has its own test software AND supervisory software
- Supervisory software must integrate across all modules
- MES/traceability integration required
- QR Code-driven recipe selection
- Real-time data acquisition and result recording
- PLC controls product via **RS-485/Modbus** directly (confirmed by Julio)
- HMI test: NOT required (per Julio's clarification with PIA Automation)
- USB test: pending decision

---

## Hardware requirements (customer-supplied)

- **WEG PLC500** (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- **WEG portfolio components** (drives, inverters, safety hardware) — supplied by **WAU**
- **WAU electronic loads** (for load test module)

---

## Key constraints and requirements

- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- **NR10 and NR12** Brazilian safety standards compliance (interlocks, emergency buttons, movable guards)
- IEC standards compliance
- **FAT** (Factory Acceptance Test) + **SAT** (Site Acceptance Test) + startup
- Complete documentation: mechanical/electrical design files, CAD (SolidWorks 2022 or Parasolid), 2D PDF drawings
- Technical manual (description, operating instructions, warranty, drawings, lifting process, component list, maintenance instructions)
- **ART** (Anotação de Responsabilidade Técnica) — mechanical and electrical — required
- Preventive maintenance schedule
- Operational and maintenance training
- Spare parts availability
- On-site and remote technical support options
- Virtual simulation or prototyping for validation
- **Global replication** potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — concept designed for global standardization
- "Silence = full compliance" clause: **mandatory to flag any deviation** in quotation

---

## Three quotes requested from each supplier

1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only (separate project — Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)
3. Full integrated line

---

## Documentation folder

`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\` — all project documentation lives here:
- Scope document (PT and EN versions)
- Product specs: CFW900, CFW500-501, CFW11, TABELA TENSÕES E CORRENTES
- Simulation images
- 3D product models (SolidWorks .sldasm)
- Supplier meeting summaries (ATAS folder)
- SUPPLIERS.xlsx tracker

**Why:** This is Aluizio's primary work project. All supplier evaluation, email drafting, meeting preparation, and technical documentation relates to this scope.

**How to apply:** When Aluizio asks about the project, always consult this memory. For detailed product/connection specs, refer to the files in `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\ORION and similars power and mechanical specifications\`.

---

## Product spec files (current coverage)

- `TABELA TENSOES E CORRENTES ORION.xlsx` — Full table: model code, mechanical size (A/B/C), current (A), voltage (V), power (VA/W)
  - Current range: 2.2A to 60A+

**Project name:** Modular All Drives Test System

**Purpose:** End-of-line (EOL) automated testing system for WEG variable frequency drives (VFDs/frequency inverters) production line.

**Installation site:** WEG facility in Jaraguá do Sul, Santa Catarina, Brazil.

**Deadline:** End of 2027 — commissioned in Brazil.

**Three test modules:**
1. **Hipot test** — dielectric/insulation test, max 4 kV, ~3–60 seconds per unit depending on drive
2. **Functional test** — validates current/voltage measurements (tolerance <10%), HMI operation via RS-485/Modbus (no HMI test needed per Julio), takes ~1.5–7 min
3. **Load test** — 50°C heated cabin (temperature only, no humidity control), ~6–20 min per unit; uses electronic loads up to 60A at 220/440/575V; fire suppression flagged by multiple suppliers as needed

**Architecture:**
- Modular conveyor system with free-flow pallet function (product pulled aside for test while line stays free)
- Automatic electrical connections (docking) — ~60A power connections; automatic connection only works if drive connectors are designed for automated mating
- One test position per module
- Parallelization: 1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load test modules
- Assembly line is a SEPARATE project (executed by Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)

**Software requirements:**
- Open-source languages ONLY: C#, Java, or Python — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery mandatory
- MES/traceability integration required
- PLC controls product via RS-485/Modbus directly

**Hardware requirements (customer-supplied):**
- WEG PLC500 (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- WEG portfolio components (drives, inverters, safety hardware)
- WAU electronic loads

**Key constraints:**
- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- NR10/NR12 Brazilian safety standards compliance
- FAT (Factory Acceptance Test) + SAT (Site Acceptance Test) required
- Global replication potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — project can be replicated globally (useful commercial argument with suppliers)
- "Silence = full compliance" clause in RFQ (suppliers must explicitly list deviations)

**Three quotes requested from each supplier:**
1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only
3. Full integrated line

**Why:** Modernize VFD production testing, achieve full automation, global replication capability.

**How to apply:** This is Aluizio's primary work project. All supplier evaluation, email drafting, meeting preparation, and technical documentation relates to this scope.

## Products covered

- **Line:** CFW series (Orion and similars) — mechanical sizes A, B, C
- **Naming convention:** CFWn00A/B/C followed by current and voltage code
- **Current range:** 2.2A to 60A+
- **Voltages:** 220V, 440V, 575V
- **Max product dimensions (fixture must accommodate):** H×D×W = 460mm × 300mm × 220mm
- **Max product weight:** 30 kg
- **Models include:** CFW900, CFW500/501, CFW11 and Orion variants

---

## System architecture (visual concept)

The system is a conveyor-based modular line. Each module is a self-contained enclosure (WEG-branded, tower light, polycarbonate windows) with:
- **Side door** that opens automatically when fixture arrives
- Fixture with product slides **into the rear** of the module
- **Automatic electrical connections** are made at the rear of the module (docking)
- Test is performed inside
- Fixture exits to the right, returns to conveyor
- Each module has its own return conveyor at the lower level

**Full line flow:**
Assembly → Hipot Test → Functional Test → Load Test (Heated) → Packaging

---

## Three test modules

### 1. Hipot Test Module (Tensão Aplicada)
- Dielectric test — validates electrical insulation of power connections
- Insulation level: **up to 4 kV** (wiring rated for 4kV to avoid leakage/noise)
- Cycle time: **< 1 minute**
- Safety enclosure with monitored access; automatic grounding; discharge circuits
- Automatic prevention of door opening during high voltage application
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading

### 2. Functional Test Module
- Complete electrical, logical and operational validation
- Cycle time: **< 5 minutes**
- Controlled by **WEG PLC500** (mandatory, non-negotiable)
- HMI: computer or touch screen for monitoring, results, manual test execution, new product registration
- Integrated **computer vision system** for HMI inspection and optical validation
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading
- **Test sequence (sequential, automatic):**
  1. External control energization; HMI measurement and operation validation
  2. Power section energization; link measurement, control power supplies validation
  3. Firmware version verification
  4. I/Os
  5. Serial number programming
  6. MAC address programming
  7. Ethernet ports
  8. USB port
  9. Internal battery validation
  10. RS-485 communication
  11. Motor operation and output current measurement
  12. Safety stop module validation
  13. Rheostatic braking
  14. Ground fault and output short-circuit protection tests
  15. Factory default parameter programming

### 3. Load Test Module (Heated)
- Stress test under controlled load and temperature
- Cycle time: **< 20 minutes**
- Heated cabin: **controllable up to 50°C** (temperature only — no humidity control)
- Electronic loads supplied by **WAU** (WEG group company)
- Supports currents up to **60A** and voltages **220V, 440V, 575V**
- Real-time data acquisition; recipe-driven

---

## Fixtures

- **5 types** × 3 units per type = **15 fixtures total**
- All fixtures have **standardized external dimensions** — only the mechanical nest varies per product type
- QR Code identification on each fixture
- Mechanical **Poka-Yoke** for product positioning
- Must support products up to 30 kg without deformation
- Pre-wired with connectors/harnesses connected to the product
- Fixture contact pads/connectors at rear for automatic docking connection
- Material: resistant, with lifting/handling points
- Fixture return conveyor on **lower level** of modules

---

## General module specifications

- Power supply: **380 Vac, 60 Hz**
- Structure: aluminum profile frame + **5mm polycarbonate** enclosure/doors
- Base: reinforced beams **100mm high** for manual pallet truck transport + flanges
- All cabin materials: **non-combustible** (or flame-retardant + non-toxic); manufacturer report required
- Structure prevents body access into cabin with doors closed
- **1 test position per module** (no parallel positions within one module)
- Parallelization is achieved by adding more modules (1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load modules)
- Automatic side doors for fixture passage between modules
- Computer vision system for optical inspection
- Transparent windows for full product visibility during tests
- Easy access for mechanical, electrical and pneumatic maintenance
- Fire suppression system (flagged by multiple suppliers as necessary — not yet formally in scope as of June 2026)

---

## Operational flow (26 steps)

1. Product assembled on fixture and connected
2. Automatic QR Code reading at assembly stage
3. Automatic recipe selection
4. Automatic movement to hipot module via conveyor
5. Automatic movement to test position
6. Electrical/mechanical connection
7. Test execution
8. Data recording
9. Approval/rejection
10. Return to conveyor
11–17. Functional test (same sequence as above)
18–24. Load test (same sequence)
25. Final segregation: approved → packaging; rejected → repair/analysis
26. Fixtures return via lower return conveyor

**Rejected products** stay on line with rejection flag in supervisory database — segregated at end of line.

---

## Software requirements

- **Languages: C#, Java, or Python ONLY** — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery **mandatory**
- Each module has its own test software AND supervisory software
- Supervisory software must integrate across all modules
- MES/traceability integration required
- QR Code-driven recipe selection
- Real-time data acquisition and result recording
- PLC controls product via **RS-485/Modbus** directly (confirmed by Julio)
- HMI test: NOT required (per Julio's clarification with PIA Automation)
- USB test: pending decision

---

## Hardware requirements (customer-supplied)

- **WEG PLC500** (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- **WEG portfolio components** (drives, inverters, safety hardware) — supplied by **WAU**
- **WAU electronic loads** (for load test module)

---

## Key constraints and requirements

- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- **NR10 and NR12** Brazilian safety standards compliance (interlocks, emergency buttons, movable guards)
- IEC standards compliance
- **FAT** (Factory Acceptance Test) + **SAT** (Site Acceptance Test) + startup
- Complete documentation: mechanical/electrical design files, CAD (SolidWorks 2022 or Parasolid), 2D PDF drawings
- Technical manual (description, operating instructions, warranty, drawings, lifting process, component list, maintenance instructions)
- **ART** (Anotação de Responsabilidade Técnica) — mechanical and electrical — required
- Preventive maintenance schedule
- Operational and maintenance training
- Spare parts availability
- On-site and remote technical support options
- Virtual simulation or prototyping for validation
- **Global replication** potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — concept designed for global standardization
- "Silence = full compliance" clause: **mandatory to flag any deviation** in quotation

---

## Three quotes requested from each supplier

1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only (separate project — Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)
3. Full integrated line

---

## Documentation folder

`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\` — all project documentation lives here:
- Scope document (PT and EN versions)
- Product specs: CFW900, CFW500-501, CFW11, TABELA TENSÕES E CORRENTES
- Simulation images
- 3D product models (SolidWorks .sldasm)
- Supplier meeting summaries (ATAS folder)
- SUPPLIERS.xlsx tracker

**Why:** This is Aluizio's primary work project. All supplier evaluation, email drafting, meeting preparation, and technical documentation relates to this scope.

**How to apply:** When Aluizio asks about the project, always consult this memory. For detailed product/connection specs, refer to the files in `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\ORION and similars power and mechanical specifications\`.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user (Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner, WEG Drives & Controls) wanted to transfer knowledge from past Claude.ai conversations into the current Claude Code session's memory system. The sequence of requests was:
   - Share 9 Claude.ai conversation links → failed (JS-rendered, auth-required pages)
   - Point to `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\CLAUDE CHATS` with 9 exported PDFs → successfully read
   - Point to `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION` as the authoritative project documentation folder → read scope, definitions, images, product specs
   - Correct key naming errors in memory (Orion = CFW600, system name = Modular All Drives, full product scope)
   - Ask whether the MEETING TRANSCRIPTIONS folder had been read → it had not; extraction was started but reading was not yet completed

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Modular All Drives Test System** — EOL automated VFD test line with 3 independent modules
   - **Orion = CFW600** — new WEG product (mechanical sizes A, B, C); when Aluizio says "Orion" he means CFW600
   - **Products tested:** CFW600 (A,B,C), CFW900 (A,B,C,D), CFW11 (A,B,C,D), CFW500 (D,E), and similars
   - **Three test modules:** Hipot (dielectric, ≤4kV, <1 min), Functional (PLC500, 15 tests, <5 min), Load (heated 50°C, WAU electronic loads, <20 min)
   - **Hard constraints:** WEG PLC500 only (no Siemens), C#/Java/Python only (no LabVIEW), source code delivery mandatory, Brazil commissioning and support required, NR10/NR12 compliance
   - **Fixtures:** 5 types × 3 units = 15 total; standardized external dims; QR code ID; Poka-Yoke; rear docking for automatic electrical connection; max product 460×300×220mm, 30kg
   - **Supplier pipeline:** 8 suppliers met; JR Automation declined; BBS Automation #1 in Pugh matrix (WEG model); Bosch best for Brazil support
   - **Memory system** at `C:\Users\aluizio\.claude\projects\c--Projetos-PROJETOS-ats-f03\memory\`
   - **pdftotext** (Git install) used to extract PDFs; **docx2txt** (Git install) used to extract DOCX files
   - **Notion connector** available but write actions require user approval in UI; read/search works freely
   - **Fireflies** used for meeting transcription → standard output is a structured Word document

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **Memory files created/updated:**
   - `C:\Users\aluizio\.claude\projects\c--Projetos-PROJETOS-ats-f03\memory\MEMORY.md` — index of all memory files
   - `memory\user_profile.md` — Aluizio's role, WEG context, English level (C1), phone (+55 47 99268-6220), Notion/Fireflies tools
   - `memory\project_modular_all_drives.md` — Full project spec: ORION folder codename, 3 modules with full specs, 15-step functional test sequence, 26-step operational flow, fixture specs, software/hardware requirements, supplier quote format (3 separate quotes), documentation CAD requirements (SolidWorks 2022)
   - `memory\suppliers_status.md` — All 8 suppliers, meeting dates, status, open actions per supplier, Pugh matrix rankings
   - `memory\masters_degree.md` — PPGESE/UFSC Joinville, Data Science (ESE410031), assessment dates Aug–Sep 2026, Wine EDA notebook completed
   - `memory\feedback_writing_preferences.md` — No travessões, accents required, direct email style, English correction patterns, Notion/Fireflies workflow
   - `memory\reference_orion_folder.md` — Pointer to `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\` as authoritative project docs

   **Source documents read:**
