**Root folder:** `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\`

This is the authoritative source for everything about the [[project-modular-all-drives]] project.

**Note on the folder name:** "ORION" refers to the CFW600 product (Aluizio's shorthand). The test *system* inside is called "Modular All Drives." Meeting transcriptions from supplier calls will be added here by Aluizio — process them into structured Word documents when asked.

## Key files

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `Escopo Sistema de Testes Modular All Drives.pdf/.docx` | Full project scope in Portuguese (Revision 00, May 2026) |
| `Modular All Drives Test System Definitions .pdf/.docx` | English version of the scope/definitions |
| `Orion Test Systems Presentation .pptx` | Project presentation |

## Subfolders

| Folder | Contents |
|--------|----------|
| `ORION and similars power and mechanical specifications\` | Product electrical specs: CFW900.xlsx, CFW500-501.xlsx, CFW11.xlsx, TABELA TENSOES E CORRENTES ORION.xlsx, Solic Aluizio.xlsx, power connections PDF, tabela f03f13f11.xlsm |
| `IMAGENS SIMULAÇÃO MODULAR ALL DRIVES\` | Concept simulation images and AI-generated videos |
| `PRODUTOS EM 3D\` | SolidWorks .sldasm files for Mec A/B/C IP20 and Mec B/D IP66 |
| `SUPPLIERS\` | SUPPLIERS.xlsx tracker + ATAS subfolder with meeting summaries (DOCX) for DMC, BBS, MASMEC, Bosch, Develop LLC, PIA Automation |
| `CLAUDE CHATS\` | Exported PDF conversations (already read and saved to memory) |

## Product spec files (current coverage)

- `CFW900.xlsx` — CFW900 series specs
- `CFW500-501.xlsx` — CFW500/501 series specs
- `CFW11.xlsx` — CFW11 series specs
- `TABELA TENSOES E CORRENTES ORION.xlsx` — Full table: model code, mechanical size (A/B/C), current (A), voltage (V), power (VA/W)
  - Current range: 2.2A to 60A+
  - Voltages: 220V, 440V, 575V (T2=220V, T4=440V, T5/T6=575V in model codes)
  - Mechanical sizes: A (smallest), B, C (largest)

---
name: project-modular-all-drives
description: "Aluizio's main work project — automated EOL test system for VFD (frequency inverter) production at WEG. Internal codename ORION."
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: f53aa9c9-e9fa-4976-8c10-5fea6574d0be
---

**Project name:** Modular All Drives Test System
**Project folder:** 2026-04-ORION (named after the Orion/CFW600 product that created this opportunity)
**Document author:** Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner, Revision 00, May/2026

**Purpose:** End-of-line (EOL) automated testing system for WEG variable frequency drives (VFDs). The launch of the new **Orion (CFW600)** product created the opportunity to build this new modular test concept that covers multiple WEG drive families.

**IMPORTANT — naming:** When Aluizio says "Orion" he means **CFW600** (the new WEG product). The test *system* is called **Modular All Drives**. Do not confuse them.

**Installation site:** WEG Drives & Controls — Automation Ltd., Drives Production Department, Jaraguá do Sul, Santa Catarina, Brazil.

**Deadline:** End of 2027 — commissioned in Brazil.

---

## Products to be tested (all families)

| Family | Mechanical sizes |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Orion (CFW600)** | A, B, C |
| **CFW900** | A, B, C, D |
| **CFW11** | A, B, C, D |
| **CFW500** | D, E |
| Similars | TBD |

- **Current range:** 2.2A to 60A+
- **Voltages:** 220V, 440V, 575V
- **Max product dimensions (fixture must accommodate):** H×D×W = 460mm × 300mm × 220mm
- **Max product weight:** 30 kg
- In the TABELA TENSOES E CORRENTES ORION.xlsx, product codes appear as "CFWn00A/B/C" — the "n" is a placeholder representing the product family number (e.g. n=6 for CFW600)

---

## System architecture (visual concept)

The system is a conveyor-based modular line. Each module is a self-contained enclosure (WEG-branded, tower light, polycarbonate windows) with:
- **Side door** that opens automatically when fixture arrives
- Fixture with product slides **into the rear** of the module
- **Automatic electrical connections** are made at the rear of the module (docking)
- Test is performed inside
- Fixture exits to the right, returns to conveyor
- Each module has its own return conveyor at the lower level

**Full line flow:**
Assembly → Hipot Test → Functional Test → Load Test (Heated) → Packaging

---

## Three test modules

### 1. Hipot Test Module (Tensão Aplicada)
- Dielectric test — validates electrical insulation of power connections
- Insulation level: **up to 4 kV** (wiring rated for 4kV to avoid leakage/noise)
- Cycle time: **< 1 minute**
- Safety enclosure with monitored access; automatic grounding; discharge circuits
- Automatic prevention of door opening during high voltage application
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading

### 2. Functional Test Module
- Complete electrical, logical and operational validation
- Cycle time: **< 5 minutes**
- Controlled by **WEG PLC500** (mandatory, non-negotiable)
- HMI: computer or touch screen for monitoring, results, manual test execution, new product registration
- Integrated **computer vision system** for HMI inspection and optical validation
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading
- **Test sequence (sequential, automatic):**
  1. External control energization; HMI measurement and operation validation
  2. Power section energization; link measurement, control power supplies validation
  3. Firmware version verification
  4. I/Os
  5. Serial number programming
  6. MAC address programming
  7. Ethernet ports
  8. USB port
  9. Internal battery validation
  10. RS-485 communication
  11. Motor operation and output current measurement
  12. Safety stop module validation
  13. Rheostatic braking
  14. Ground fault and output short-circuit protection tests
  15. Factory default parameter programming

### 3. Load Test Module (Heated)
- Stress test under controlled load and temperature
- Cycle time: **< 20 minutes**
- Heated cabin: **controllable up to 50°C** (temperature only — no humidity control)
- Electronic loads supplied by **WAU** (WEG group company)
- Supports currents up to **60A** and voltages **220V, 440V, 575V**
- Real-time data acquisition; recipe-driven

---

## Fixtures

- **5 types** × 3 units per type = **15 fixtures total**
- All fixtures have **standardized external dimensions** — only the mechanical nest varies per product type
- QR Code identification on each fixture
- Mechanical **Poka-Yoke** for product positioning
- Must support products up to 30 kg without deformation
- Pre-wired with connectors/harnesses connected to the product
- Fixture contact pads/connectors at rear for automatic docking connection
- Material: resistant, with lifting/handling points
- Fixture return conveyor on **lower level** of modules

---

## General module specifications

- Power supply: **380 Vac, 60 Hz**
- Structure: aluminum profile frame + **5mm polycarbonate** enclosure/doors
- Base: reinforced beams **100mm high** for manual pallet truck transport + flanges
- All cabin materials: **non-combustible** (or flame-retardant + non-toxic); manufacturer report required
- Structure prevents body access into cabin with doors closed
- **1 test position per module** (no parallel positions within one module)
- Parallelization is achieved by adding more modules (1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load modules)
- Automatic side doors for fixture passage between modules
- Computer vision system for optical inspection
- Transparent windows for full product visibility during tests
- Easy access for mechanical, electrical and pneumatic maintenance
- Fire suppression system (flagged by multiple suppliers as necessary — not yet formally in scope as of June 2026)

---

## Operational flow (26 steps)

1. Product assembled on fixture and connected
2. Automatic QR Code reading at assembly stage
3. Automatic recipe selection
4. Automatic movement to hipot module via conveyor
5. Automatic movement to test position
6. Electrical/mechanical connection
7. Test execution
8. Data recording
9. Approval/rejection
10. Return to conveyor
11–17. Functional test (same sequence as above)
18–24. Load test (same sequence)
25. Final segregation: approved → packaging; rejected → repair/analysis
26. Fixtures return via lower return conveyor

**Rejected products** stay on line with rejection flag in supervisory database — segregated at end of line.

---

## Software requirements

- **Languages: C#, Java, or Python ONLY** — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery **mandatory**
- Each module has its own test software AND supervisory software
- Supervisory software must integrate across all modules
- MES/traceability integration required
- QR Code-driven recipe selection
- Real-time data acquisition and result recording
- PLC controls product via **RS-485/Modbus** directly (confirmed by Julio)
- HMI test: NOT required (per Julio's clarification with PIA Automation)
- USB test: pending decision

---

## Hardware requirements (customer-supplied)

- **WEG PLC500** (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- **WEG portfolio components** (drives, inverters, safety hardware) — supplied by **WAU**
- **WAU electronic loads** (for load test module)

---

## Key constraints and requirements

- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- **NR10 and NR12** Brazilian safety standards compliance (interlocks, emergency buttons, movable guards)
- IEC standards compliance
- **FAT** (Factory Acceptance Test) + **SAT** (Site Acceptance Test) + startup
- Complete documentation: mechanical/electrical design files, CAD (SolidWorks 2022 or Parasolid), 2D PDF drawings
- Technical manual (description, operating instructions, warranty, drawings, lifting process, component list, maintenance instructions)
- **ART** (Anotação de Responsabilidade Técnica) — mechanical and electrical — required
- Preventive maintenance schedule
- Operational and maintenance training
- Spare parts availability
- On-site and remote technical support options
- Virtual simulation or prototyping for validation
- **Global replication** potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — concept designed for global standardization
- "Silence = full compliance" clause: **mandatory to flag any deviation** in quotation

---

## Three quotes requested from each supplier

1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only (separate project — Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)
3. Full integrated line

---

## Documentation folder

`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\` — all project documentation lives here:
- Scope document (PT and EN versions)
- Product specs: CFW900, CFW500-501, CFW11, TABELA TENSÕES E CORRENTES
- Simulation images
- 3D product models (SolidWorks .sldasm)
- Supplier meeting summaries (ATAS folder)
- SUPPLIERS.xlsx tracker

**Why:** This is Aluizio's primary work project. All supplier evaluation, email drafting, meeting preparation, and technical documentation relates to this scope.

**How to apply:** When Aluizio asks about the project, always consult this memory. For detailed product/connection specs, refer to the files in `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\ORION and similars power and mechanical specifications\`.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

content.thinking:
Now I've read all 6 transcriptions. Let me extract the key information from each and update the suppliers_status.md memory file. Let me also check the current state of that file.

Key information from each meeting:

**DMC (USA) — June 17, 2026, 52 min**
- Contacts: Brent Hoerman (Senior Engineering Manager), Neal Walters (Mechanical Engineering Lead), Aleks Konstantinovic (Automation engineer)
- DMC is Chicago-based, ~250-300 employees, 14-15 regional US offices, 25,000 sqft manufacturing center
- Specializes in test/measurement and manufacturing automation with expertise in conveyor, palletizing, and robotics systems
- Transitioning from LabVIEW-heavy to a 50-50 split, willing to use C#, Java, and Python as needed
- Planning FAT in Chicago followed by engineer deployment to Brazil for SAT and startup
- Open to non-WEG PLCs for the initial quote with WEG components discussed later
- Key technical concerns center on connection strategy (pogo pins versus robot interface), available space for test electronics, and whether a separate rack is needed
- Quote expected after the July 4th holiday, targeting late June or early July
- Architecture has PC as primary controller with PLC serving as secondary HMI and operator interface
- Flagged concerns about automated connection reliability
- Needs CAD models of the product connectors to move forward
- Also speaking with BBS Automation in China (part of Dual Group since 2023), which operates 20 global sites with 20,500 employees and ~200M EUR revenue in China alone across multiple factories
- BBS typically uses Siemens PLCs but has concerns about compatibility with WEG PLC500 and will need to verify internally
- They have test and assembly capabilities, formerly under the Team Technique brand for test software, and normally rely on LabVIEW but have a C# alternative through Team Soft
- Will commission in Brazil pending invitation letters from WEG, open to including WEG components in quotes, though concerned about timeline and PLC familiarity
- Needs just pricing for the first round with three quotes required: test line, assembly line, and full package, with supplier selection targeted for end of 2026 and commissioning by end of 2027

Also connecting with MASMEC in Italy, a 300-person operation in Bari with €50M revenue target and 84% focused on automation for automotive — they've already built inverter testing lines with hipot, functional, and load testing capabilities

. They offer both manual and automatic connection solutions using Bosch Rexroth TS2 conveyors with flow-through capability, defaulting to Siemens PLC but flexible on programming (LabVIEW or C#), and have digital twin simulation experience with Plant Simulation software. They're comfortable commissioning in Brazil using local partners for immediate support while MASMEC engineers handle warranty work, will create a 3D concept during the proposal phase, and are open to including all WEG components — their R&D team will dive into load test specifics, and they've acknowledged CREATIC as a contact supplier for specialized contactors, with timeline feasibility confirmed for both assembly and testing.

Now looking at Bosch's Brazil operation — they're a major player with 60+ years in manufacturing innovation across their Campinas and Curitiba facilities, supplying both internal Bosch plants and external customers. They have solid assembly line experience with ECUs and PCB manipulation similar to what we need, know WEG products intimately (they have an exclusive contract and purchase over a million WEG products annually), and have already delivered five automation solutions to WEG Controles.

They're planning to use Bosch Rexroth conveyors imported from Germany with local build and commissioning in Brazil, while Bosch handles the SCADA system. They need detailed test prescriptions for each product type rather than just an overview, and Victor raised a question about whether the WEG PLC500 has sufficient performance or if we need something higher-spec. They're flexible on WEG components across the board, want to visit our factory or have us visit theirs, and are targeting 900 drives per day at full production speed by year five, with commissioning by end of 2027 and supplier selection happening July-August 2026.

Met with IA Automation from China on June 18th for a brief conversation without a formal presentation. They've done automated assembly lines and robotics work, including a previous Brazil project with connector assembly equipment near the capital. They confirmed they can handle site commissioning and production support in Brazil. We clarified several technical points: they need to know the contact group count for hipot testing on power connections only, max voltage is 4kV, load test temperature is fixed at 50°C, HMI testing isn't required since the PLC communicates via RS485/Modbus with the product, and USB port testing will need a computer as host which Julio would provide pending a decision. They prefer WEG for the PLC but are open to alternatives.

They're heading into a three-day holiday period starting June 18th, so the quote should come through next week. This was a quick meeting and we'll need deeper technical discussions to follow up.

Also spoke with Matt Moseman from Develop LLC in Wisconsin, just north of Chicago. They're strictly North America focused and explicitly cannot commission or support work in Brazil—they'd want site acceptance testing done stateside. They're open to potentially building a dedicated team in Brazil at WEG's expense, though they currently have no presence or partners in that region. They have solid PCB and electronics assembly experience from the lighting industry, including precision plastic and electronics work, and machine building aligns well with the project scope. They're looking at a Q1 2027 start with end-of-2027 delivery being feasible.

The key constraint is that any Brazil commissioning or support requires WEG to fund a dedicated local team. Most of their projects are under NDA so references aren't available. Supplier selection wraps up end of 2026.

A few important things came up across all the meetings: fire suppression is now on the radar—Matt raised it and Aluizio confirmed they hadn't considered it initially but suppliers flagged it as necessary, so it needs to be added to scope. USB testing requires a computer host and that decision is still pending. HMI testing actually isn't required since the PLC communicates via RS485/Modbus. The production target is 900 drives per day by year five based on what came out of the Bosch meeting.

For suppliers, the down-select happens July-August 2026 with purchase orders at year-end. Alissonn already has the assembly line scope mapped out with a cycle time around 9 minutes, and the CFW600 design minimizes cables almost entirely. The Bosch Rexroth TS2 conveyor is the standard choice for this weight and size. Both Develop and DMC said they're flexible on the PLC and open to using WEG's PLC500. The main risk is Develop—they can't do any Brazil commissioning without additional cost.

Now I need to update the suppliers status file and project memory with these new insights.

---
name: project-modular-all-drives
description: "Aluizio's main work project — automated EOL test system for VFD (frequency inverter) production at WEG. Internal codename ORION."
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: f53aa9c9-e9fa-4976-8c10-5fea6574d0be
---

**Project name:** Modular All Drives Test System
**Project folder:** 2026-04-ORION (named after the Orion/CFW600 product that created this opportunity)
**Document author:** Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner, Revision 00, May/2026

**Purpose:** End-of-line (EOL) automated testing system for WEG variable frequency drives (VFDs). The launch of the new **Orion (CFW600)** product created the opportunity to build this new modular test concept that covers multiple WEG drive families.

**IMPORTANT — naming:** When Aluizio says "Orion" he means **CFW600** (the new WEG product). The test *system* is called **Modular All Drives**. Do not confuse them.

**Installation site:** WEG Drives & Controls — Automation Ltd., Drives Production Department, Jaraguá do Sul, Santa Catarina, Brazil.

**Deadline:** End of 2027 — commissioned in Brazil.

---

## Products to be tested (all families)

| Family | Mechanical sizes |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Orion (CFW600)** | A, B, C |
| **CFW900** | A, B, C, D |
| **CFW11** | A, B, C, D |
| **CFW500** | D, E |
| Similars | TBD |

- **Current range:** 2.2A to 60A+
- **Voltages:** 220V, 440V, 575V
- **Max product dimensions (fixture must accommodate):** H×D×W = 460mm × 300mm × 220mm
- **Max product weight:** 30 kg
- In the TABELA TENSOES E CORRENTES ORION.xlsx, product codes appear as "CFWn00A/B/C" — the "n" is a placeholder representing the product family number (e.g. n=6 for CFW600)

---

## System architecture (visual concept)

The system is a conveyor-based modular line. Each module is a self-contained enclosure (WEG-branded, tower light, polycarbonate windows) with:
- **Side door** that opens automatically when fixture arrives
- Fixture with product slides **into the rear** of the module
- **Automatic electrical connections** are made at the rear of the module (docking)
- Test is performed inside
- Fixture exits to the right, returns to conveyor
- Each module has its own return conveyor at the lower level

**Full line flow:**
Assembly → Hipot Test → Functional Test → Load Test (Heated) → Packaging

---

## Three test modules

### 1. Hipot Test Module (Tensão Aplicada)
- Dielectric test — validates electrical insulation of power connections
- Insulation level: **up to 4 kV** (wiring rated for 4kV to avoid leakage/noise)
- Cycle time: **< 1 minute**
- Safety enclosure with monitored access; automatic grounding; discharge circuits
- Automatic prevention of door opening during high voltage application
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading

### 2. Functional Test Module
- Complete electrical, logical and operational validation
- Cycle time: **< 5 minutes**
- Controlled by **WEG PLC500** (mandatory, non-negotiable)
- HMI: computer or touch screen for monitoring, results, manual test execution, new product registration
- Integrated **computer vision system** for HMI inspection and optical validation
- QR Code reading → automatic recipe loading
- **Test sequence (sequential, automatic):**
  1. External control energization; HMI measurement and operation validation
  2. Power section energization; link measurement, control power supplies validation
  3. Firmware version verification
  4. I/Os
  5. Serial number programming
  6. MAC address programming
  7. Ethernet ports
  8. USB port
  9. Internal battery validation
  10. RS-485 communication
  11. Motor operation and output current measurement
  12. Safety stop module validation
  13. Rheostatic braking
  14. Ground fault and output short-circuit protection tests
  15. Factory default parameter programming

### 3. Load Test Module (Heated)
- Stress test under controlled load and temperature
- Cycle time: **< 20 minutes**
- Heated cabin: **controllable up to 50°C** (temperature only — no humidity control)
- Electronic loads supplied by **WAU** (WEG group company)
- Supports currents up to **60A** and voltages **220V, 440V, 575V**
- Real-time data acquisition; recipe-driven

---

## Fixtures

- **5 types** × 3 units per type = **15 fixtures total**
- All fixtures have **standardized external dimensions** — only the mechanical nest varies per product type
- QR Code identification on each fixture
- Mechanical **Poka-Yoke** for product positioning
- Must support products up to 30 kg without deformation
- Pre-wired with connectors/harnesses connected to the product
- Fixture contact pads/connectors at rear for automatic docking connection
- Material: resistant, with lifting/handling points
- Fixture return conveyor on **lower level** of modules

---

## General module specifications

- Power supply: **380 Vac, 60 Hz**
- Structure: aluminum profile frame + **5mm polycarbonate** enclosure/doors
- Base: reinforced beams **100mm high** for manual pallet truck transport + flanges
- All cabin materials: **non-combustible** (or flame-retardant + non-toxic); manufacturer report required
- Structure prevents body access into cabin with doors closed
- **1 test position per module** (no parallel positions within one module)
- Parallelization is achieved by adding more modules (1 hipot + 1 functional can feed 3–5 load modules)
- Automatic side doors for fixture passage between modules
- Computer vision system for optical inspection
- Transparent windows for full product visibility during tests
- Easy access for mechanical, electrical and pneumatic maintenance
- Fire suppression system (flagged by multiple suppliers as necessary — not yet formally in scope as of June 2026)

---

## Operational flow (26 steps)

1. Product assembled on fixture and connected
2. Automatic QR Code reading at assembly stage
3. Automatic recipe selection
4. Automatic movement to hipot module via conveyor
5. Automatic movement to test position
6. Electrical/mechanical connection
7. Test execution
8. Data recording
9. Approval/rejection
10. Return to conveyor
11–17. Functional test (same sequence as above)
18–24. Load test (same sequence)
25. Final segregation: approved → packaging; rejected → repair/analysis
26. Fixtures return via lower return conveyor

**Rejected products** stay on line with rejection flag in supervisory database — segregated at end of line.

---

## Software requirements

- **Languages: C#, Java, or Python ONLY** — LabVIEW explicitly NOT accepted
- Source code delivery **mandatory**
- Each module has its own test software AND supervisory software
- Supervisory software must integrate across all modules
- MES/traceability integration required
- QR Code-driven recipe selection
- Real-time data acquisition and result recording
- PLC controls product via **RS-485/Modbus** directly (confirmed by Julio)
- HMI test: NOT required (per Julio's clarification with PIA Automation)
- USB test: pending decision

---

## Hardware requirements (customer-supplied)

- **WEG PLC500** (Siemens PLCs explicitly NOT accepted — WEG produces its own PLCs/drives)
- **WEG portfolio components** (drives, inverters, safety hardware) — supplied by **WAU**
- **WAU electronic loads** (for load test module)

---

## Key constraints and requirements

- Brazil commissioning and ongoing support required (deal-breaker for many overseas suppliers)
- **NR10 and NR12** Brazilian safety standards compliance (interlocks, emergency buttons, movable guards)
- IEC standards compliance
- **FAT** (Factory Acceptance Test) + **SAT** (Site Acceptance Test) + startup
- Complete documentation: mechanical/electrical design files, CAD (SolidWorks 2022 or Parasolid), 2D PDF drawings
- Technical manual (description, operating instructions, warranty, drawings, lifting process, component list, maintenance instructions)
- **ART** (Anotação de Responsabilidade Técnica) — mechanical and electrical — required
- Preventive maintenance schedule
- Operational and maintenance training
- Spare parts availability
- On-site and remote technical support options
- Virtual simulation or prototyping for validation
- **Global replication** potential: WEG has 60+ plants worldwide — concept designed for global standardization
- "Silence = full compliance" clause: **mandatory to flag any deviation** in quotation

---

## Three quotes requested from each supplier

1. Testing line only
2. Assembly line only (separate project — Franklim & Alisson/Franklin)
3. Full integrated line

---

`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\` — all project documentation lives here:

- Product specs: CFW900, CFW500-501, CFW11, TABELA TENSÕES E CORRENTES

**Meeting transcriptions processed:**

- **DMC (USA)** — Brent Hoerman, Neal Walters, Aleks. Quote after July 4th. PC is boss; PLC is secondary interface. Automated connections are highest-risk item; need CAD model. Will fly to Brazil for commissioning. No Brazil office.

- **BBS Automation (China)** — Vivian Ma, Carlson Liu. Part of Dual Group. WEG PLC500 is an uncertainty (never used it, CODESYS-based). LabVIEW default but C# possible via Team Soft (their Team Technique brand). Need invitation letters for Brazil visa. Best reference for inverter test + assembly.

- **MASMEC (Italy)** — Stefano Piccinno. Has direct automotive inverter test reference (hipot + functional + load). Uses Bosch Rexroth TS2 conveyor — natively supports flow-through. Siemens PLC default but scope is minimal; checking internally. Active load not yet confirmed. Manufactures everything in Italy.

- **Bosch Brazil** — Marcelo Laranjeira (commercial) + Victor Souza (test/electrical). Best Brazil support by far. Deep WEG partner (>1M WEG products/year purchased). Need test prescriptions per module to size the WEG PLC500. Supervisory is Bosch's own platform.

- **PIA Automation (China)** — Abel Wang, David Cui. Very brief meeting. Confirmed Brazil commissioning (prior project near Brazilian capital). Key clarification: HMI test NOT required; PLC talks to product via RS-485/Modbus.

- **Develop LLC (USA)** — Matt Moseman. Cannot commission in Brazil (North America only). Could build a dedicated Brazil team at WEG's cost. Fire suppression flagged. Technical fit is high but Brazil constraint is a potential disqualifier.

**New project facts added to memory:**
- HMI test = not required (Julio confirmed)
- USB test = pending decision
- Fire suppression = should be added to scope
- Production target = ~900 drives/day at year 5
- Bosch Rexroth TS2 = reference conveyor for this product size

This matches exactly what Matt confirmed in the meeting — Brazil is a hard constraint for Develop. Here's a draft reply:

---

**Subject:** Re: Modular All Drives — Test System Scope

Hello Matt,

Thank you for the quick response and for your transparency.

Unfortunately, commissioning and ongoing technical support in Brazil are mandatory requirements for this project. We cannot proceed with a supplier that does not have the ability to perform SAT and provide post-startup support at our facility in Jaraguá do Sul, SC.

We do not have a US facility where SAT could be performed as an alternative.

For these reasons, we will not be able to move forward with Develop LLC for this project at this time. We appreciate your interest and the constructive conversation during our meeting.

If WEG expands this concept to North American plants in the future, Develop would certainly be a strong candidate and we will keep you in mind.

Best regards,
Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner
Industrial Engineering — WEG Drives & Controls

---

$path = 'C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\SUPPLIERS.xlsx'

input.content:
# Update SUPPLIERS.xlsx with full contact info and formatting
$path = 'C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\SUPPLIERS.xlsx'

$excel = New-Object -ComObject Excel.Application
$excel.Visible = $false
$excel.DisplayAlerts = $false

$wb = $excel.Workbooks.Open($path)
$ws = $wb.Sheets.Item(1)

# Clear all existing content and formatting
$ws.Cells.Clear()

# ── COLORS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$cHeader      = 0x1E3A5F   # dark navy
$cHeaderTxt   = 0xFFFFFF   # white
$cRegionBg    = 0x2E6099   # medium blue
$cRegionTxt   = 0xFFFFFF
$cMet         = 0xD6EAD6   # light green  → met & active
$cMetTxt      = 0x1A4A1A
$cDeclined    = 0xF4CCCC   # light red    → declined/disqualified
$cDecTxt      = 0x7B1A1A
$cWaiting     = 0xFFF2CC   # light amber  → waiting for reply
$cWaitTxt     = 0x7D5A00
$cPending     = 0xF3F3F3   # light grey   → email sent, no reply
$cPendTxt     = 0x444444
$cAlt         = 0xFAFAFA   # row alternation (even rows)
$cBorder      = 0xBBBBBB

# ── COLUMN DEFINITIONS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A  Region
# B  Supplier
# C  Contact Name(s)
# D  Email(s)
# E  Outreach Status
# F  Meeting Date
# G  Pugh Rank
# H  Quote Status / Next Step
# I  Notes

# ── HEADER ROW ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$headers = @("Region","Supplier","Contact Name(s)","Email(s)","Outreach Status","Meeting Date","Pugh Rank","Quote / Next Step","Notes")
for ($c = 1; $c -le $headers.Count; $c++) {
    $cell = $ws.Cells.Item(1, $c)
    $cell.Value2 = $headers[$c-1]
    $cell.Font.Bold = $true
    $cell.Font.Size = 11
    $cell.Font.Color = $cHeaderTxt
    $cell.Interior.Color = $cHeader
    $cell.HorizontalAlignment = -4108  # xlCenter
    $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4108
    $cell.WrapText = $true
}
$ws.Rows.Item(1).RowHeight = 30

# ── DATA ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Format: Region, Name, Contacts, Emails, Status, MeetingDate, PughRank, NextStep, Notes
# Status codes: MET, DECLINED, DISQUALIFIED, WAITING, EMAIL_SENT
$data = @(
  # ── BRASIL ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("BRASIL","Bosch (Campinas/Curitiba)","Marcelo Laranjeira (Comercial)`nVictor Souza (Teste Elétrico)","Marcelo.Laranjeira@br.bosch.com","MET","17/06/2026","3º (datum)","Aguardando docs WEG: prescrições de teste + tabela de dimensões + desenhos de terminais","Melhor suporte local; >60 anos; contrato exclusivo WEG Automação (>1M prod/ano). Plataforma supervisório própria. 24/7 call center."),
  @("","Autec","","projetos@autecjoi.com.br","MET","15/06/2026","5º (-11)","Aguardando cotação","Empresa local em Jaraguá do Sul. Vantagem geográfica enorme."),
  @("","Mondragon Assembly","f.dasilveira@mondragon-assembly.com","","MET","16/06/2026","-","Aguardando cotação","Linha de montagem e teste para eletrônicos. Operação local no Brasil."),
  @("","Aumaq","","vendas@aumaqrs.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","SPI","","info@integradora.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","MCK Automação","","vendas@mckautomacao.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  # ── EUROPA ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("EUROPA","MASMEC","Stefano Piccinno (Sales Engineer)`nLuigi Di Leo","info@masmec.com","MET","16/06/2026","5º (-11)","Aguardando cotação. WEG deve: prescrição por módulo + confirmar PLC WEG (não Siemens)","Bari, Itália. Referência direta em teste de inversores automotivos (hipot, funcional, carga). Conveyor Bosch Rexroth TS2. Faz instalações no Brasil. Todas as máquinas fabricadas na Itália."),
  @("","Comau","","enviado via site","MET","11/06/2026","2º (+1)","Aguardando cotação","Fabricante automotivo grande. Presença no Brasil (Betim/MG)."),
  @("","Hahn Automation","","enviado via site","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado via site."),
  @("","ATS Corporation","","solutions@atsautomation.com","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","FFT Produktionssysteme","","info@fft.de","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  # ── EUA ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("EUA","DMC","Brent Hoerman (Senior Eng. Manager)`nNeal Walters (Mech. Eng. Lead)`nAleks Konstantinovic (Automation Eng.)","email.sales@dmcinfo.com","MET","17/06/2026","8º (-20)","Aguardando cotação (após 04/Jul). WEG deve: modelo CAD / desenho dos conectores do produto","Chicago. Especialistas em T&M + automação. C#/Java/Python confirmado. FAT em Chicago, SAT no Brasil. Sem escritório no Brasil."),
  @("","JR Automation (Hitachi)","","solutions@jrautomation.com","DECLINOU","-","-","Fora do processo","Declinou — não oferece suporte no Brasil."),
  @("","Develop LLC","Matt Moseman (Sales)","sales@develop-llc.com","DESCLASSIFICADO","15/06/2026","4º (-10)","Fora do processo — email de desclassificação enviado em 18/06/2026","Fit técnico alto. DESCLASSIFICADO: não comissiona nem suporta máquinas fora da América do Norte. WEG não possui instalação nos EUA para SAT. Manter em vista para futura expansão EUA/Canadá."),
  @("","Eclipse Automation","","enviado via site","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado via site."),
  @("","iAutomation","","customerrelations@i-automation.com","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","Acieta","","enviado via site","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado via site."),
  @("","The Morse Group","","enviado via site","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado via site."),
  # ── CHINA ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("CHINA","BBS Automation (Suzhou)","Ma Vivian (Comercial)`nLiu Carlson (Técnico)","info@bbsautomation.com`ninfo.suzhou@bbsautomation.com","MET","17/06/2026","1º (+8)","Aguardando cotação. Enviar spec de teste + escopo da linha de montagem (Alissonn). Precisará de carta-convite WEG para visto.","HQ Munich. Parte do Dual Group (2023). 4 fábricas na China. Melhor referência em teste EOL + montagem de inversores (PCB, IGBT press-fit). PLC WEG: nunca usado — verificando. Software: Team Soft (C#) disponível."),
  @("","PIA Automation (Suzhou)","Wang Abel Xiang`nCui David Qingjun","","MET","18/06/2026","7º (-15)","Aguardando cotação (~semana seguinte após feriado). WEG deve: imagens do produto (conexões) + spec de teste","Comissionamento no Brasil confirmado (já tem projeto lá — montagem automatizada de conectores). Reunião curta, sem apresentação formal. PLC: WEG preferido mas flexível."),
  @("","CASMT","","info@casmt.com","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","Ronstein","","Sales@Ronstein.com","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","Harmontronics","","info.cn@htsm.com","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  # ── JAPÃO ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("JAPÃO","Tamadic","","prir@tamadic.co.jp","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando agendamento","Email enviado. Aguardando resposta."),
  @("","Tokyo Densei","","enviado via site","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado via site.")
)

# ── WRITE DATA ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$row = 2
$lastRegion = ""
foreach ($entry in $data) {
    for ($c = 1; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $cell = $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c)
        $cell.Value2 = $entry[$c-1]
        $cell.WrapText = $true
        $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4160  # xlTop
        $cell.Font.Size = 10
    }

    # Determine row color by status
    $status = $entry[4]
    $rowColor = $cPending
    $txtColor = $cPendTxt
    if ($status -eq "MET") { $rowColor = $cMet; $txtColor = $cMetTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "DECLINOU" -or $status -eq "DESCLASSIFICADO") { $rowColor = $cDeclined; $txtColor = $cDecTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "WAITING") { $rowColor = $cWaiting; $txtColor = $cWaitTxt }

    # Color region column if new region
    $regionCell = $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1)
    if ($entry[0] -ne "") {
        $regionCell.Interior.Color = $cRegionBg
        $regionCell.Font.Color = $cRegionTxt
        $regionCell.Font.Bold = $true
        $regionCell.HorizontalAlignment = -4108
    }

    # Apply row background to cols B-I
    for ($c = 2; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Interior.Color = $rowColor
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Font.Color = $txtColor
    }

    # Bold supplier name
    $ws.Cells.Item($row, 2).Font.Bold = $true

    $row++
}

# ── ROW HEIGHTS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Set all data rows to auto-fit height
for ($r = 2; $r -le $row - 1; $r++) {
    $ws.Rows.Item($r).RowHeight = 52
}

# ── COLUMN WIDTHS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Columns.Item(1).ColumnWidth = 10   # Region
$ws.Columns.Item(2).ColumnWidth = 24   # Supplier
$ws.Columns.Item(3).ColumnWidth = 28   # Contact Names
$ws.Columns.Item(4).ColumnWidth = 36   # Emails
$ws.Columns.Item(5).ColumnWidth = 16   # Status
$ws.Columns.Item(6).ColumnWidth = 14   # Meeting Date
$ws.Columns.Item(7).ColumnWidth = 12   # Pugh Rank
$ws.Columns.Item(8).ColumnWidth = 36   # Next Step
$ws.Columns.Item(9).ColumnWidth = 48   # Notes

# ── BORDERS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$dataRange = $ws.Range("A1:I" + ($row - 1))
$borders = $dataRange.Borders
$borders.Item(7).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeLeft
$borders.Item(8).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeTop
$borders.Item(9).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeBottom
$borders.Item(10).LineStyle = 1  # xlEdgeRight
$borders.Item(11).LineStyle = 1  # xlInsideVertical
$borders.Item(12).LineStyle = 1  # xlInsideHorizontal
$borders.Item(7).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(8).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(9).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(10).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(11).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(12).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(11).Weight = 1
$borders.Item(12).Weight = 1

# ── AUTOFILTER ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Range("A1:I1").AutoFilter() | Out-Null

# ── FREEZE TOP ROW ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.SplitRow = 1
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.FreezePanes = $true

# ── LEGEND ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$legendRow = $row + 1
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Value2 = "LEGENDA"
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Font.Bold = $true

  @(1, "EMAIL ENVIADO — Sem resposta ainda", $cPending, $cPendTxt),
  @(2, "DECLINOU / DESCLASSIFICADO", $cDeclined, $cDecTxt),
  @(3, "WAITING — Aguardando resposta específica", $cWaiting, $cWaitTxt)
)
foreach ($l in $legends) {
    $lRow = $legendRow + 1 + $l[0]

    $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1).Font.Color = $l[3]

    $ws.Range("A" + $lRow + ":C" + $lRow).Merge() | Out-Null
}

# ── WRITE DATA ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$row = 2
$lastRegion = ""
foreach ($entry in $data) {
    for ($c = 1; $c -le 9; $c++) {

        $cell.WrapText = $true
        $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4160  # xlTop
        $cell.Font.Size = 10
    }

    # Determine row color by status
    $status = $entry[4]
    $rowColor = $cPending
    $txtColor = $cPendTxt
    if ($status -eq "MET") { $rowColor = $cMet; $txtColor = $cMetTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "DECLINOU" -or $status -eq "DESCLASSIFICADO") { $rowColor = $cDeclined; $txtColor = $cDecTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "WAITING") { $rowColor = $cWaiting; $txtColor = $cWaitTxt }

    # Color region column if new region
    $regionCell = $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1)

        $regionCell.Font.Color = $cRegionTxt
        $regionCell.Font.Bold = $true
        $regionCell.HorizontalAlignment = -4108
    }

    # Apply row background to cols B-I
    for ($c = 2; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Interior.Color = $rowColor

$borders.Item(12).Color = $cBorder

$borders.Item(12).Weight = 1

$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.FreezePanes = $true

$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Value2 = "LEGENDA"
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Font.Bold = $true
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Font.Size = 10

}

$titleRange.VerticalAlignment = -4108

        $cell.WrapText = $true
        $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4160
        $cell.Font.Size = 10
    }

    $status = $entry[4]
    if ($status -eq "MET") {
        $bg = $cMet; $fg = $cGreenTxt
    } elseif ($status -eq "DECLINED" -or $status -eq "DISQUALIFIED") {
        $bg = $cDeclined; $fg = $cRedTxt
    } else {
        $bg = $cPending; $fg = $cGrayTxt
    }

    # Region cell
    if ($entry[0] -ne "") {
        $rc = $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1)
        $rc.Interior.Color = $cRegionBg
        $rc.Font.Color = $cNavyTxt
        $rc.Font.Bold = $true
        $rc.HorizontalAlignment = -4108
        $rc.VerticalAlignment = -4108
    } else {
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1).Interior.Color = $cRegionBg
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1).Font.Color = $cNavyTxt
    }

    for ($c = 2; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Interior.Color = $bg
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Font.Color = $fg
    }
    $ws.Cells.Item($row, 2).Font.Bold = $true
    $ws.Rows.Item($row).RowHeight = 60
    $row++
}

# ── COLUMN WIDTHS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Columns.Item(1).ColumnWidth = 9
$ws.Columns.Item(2).ColumnWidth = 24
$ws.Columns.Item(3).ColumnWidth = 28
$ws.Columns.Item(4).ColumnWidth = 38
$ws.Columns.Item(5).ColumnWidth = 15
$ws.Columns.Item(6).ColumnWidth = 13
$ws.Columns.Item(7).ColumnWidth = 11
$ws.Columns.Item(8).ColumnWidth = 38
$ws.Columns.Item(9).ColumnWidth = 50

# ── BORDERS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$dataRange = $ws.Range("A2:I" + ($row - 1))
$b = $dataRange.Borders
for ($bi = 7; $bi -le 12; $bi++) {
    $b.Item($bi).LineStyle = 1
    $b.Item($bi).Color = $cBorder
}

# ── AUTOFILTER + FREEZE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Range("A2:I2").AutoFilter() | Out-Null
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.SplitRow = 2
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.FreezePanes = $true

# ── LEGEND ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$lr = $row + 1
$ws.Cells.Item($lr, 1).Value2 = "LEGEND"
$ws.Cells.Item($lr, 1).Font.Bold = $true
$ws.Cells.Item($lr, 1).Font.Size = 10

$leg = @(
  @("MET - Meeting held, active in process", $cMet, $cGreenTxt),
  @("EMAIL SENT - Awaiting reply", $cPending, $cGrayTxt),
  @("DECLINED / DISQUALIFIED - Out of process", $cDeclined, $cRedTxt)
)
$li = 1
foreach ($l in $leg) {
    $lRow = $lr + $li
    $lCell = $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1)
    $lCell.Value2 = $l[0]
    $lCell.Interior.Color = $l[1]
    $lCell.Font.Color = $l[2]
    $lCell.Font.Size = 9
    $lCell.Font.Bold = $true
    $ws.Range("A" + $lRow + ":C" + $lRow).Merge() | Out-Null
    $li++
}

# ── TAB COLOR ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Name = "Suppliers"
$ws.Tab.Color = $cHeader

# ── SAVE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$wb.Save()
$wb.Close($false)
$excel.Quit()
[System.Runtime.Interopservices.Marshal]::ReleaseComObject($excel) | Out-Null
Write-Host "SUPPLIERS.xlsx updated successfully."
PSEOF

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:

The user (Aluizio Augusto Kleine Kirschner, WEG Industrial Engineering) had several sequential requests in this session:

a) **Read all 6 meeting transcription files** from `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\MEETING TRANSCRIPTIONS\` (already extracted via docx2txt in the prior session) and add the knowledge to memory.

b) **Handle a Develop LLC disqualification**: User shared a screenshot of an email from Matt Moseman confirming Develop LLC does not install machines in Brazil, asking if WEG had a US facility for SAT. User said "maybe we will need to stop the quotation with him." A polite English disqualification email was drafted and confirmed by user.

c) **Update the supplier tracker HTML** (`supplier_tracker (2).html`) with the Develop LLC disqualification information.

d) **Update the SUPPLIERS.xlsx spreadsheet** with all known contact information and format it better — this is the most recent, still-in-progress task.

---

2. Key Technical Concepts:

- **Modular All Drives Test System** (WEG project, folder codename ORION): EOL automated VFD test line with 3 modules (Hipot ≤4kV, Functional <5min via WEG PLC500, Load test heated 50°C fixed <20min). Products: CFW600 A/B/C, CFW900 A/B/C/D, CFW11 A/B/C/D, CFW500 D/E.
- **Supplier evaluation**: 8 suppliers met, Pugh matrix with Bosch as datum. Quotes requested in 3 formats: test line / assembly line / full integrated line.
- **Memory system**: Files at `C:\Users\aluizio\.claude\projects\c--Projetos-PROJETOS-ats-f03\memory\`
- **PowerShell COM automation**: Used to read/write Excel .xlsx files since the Read tool cannot parse Excel natively.
- **HTML interactive tracker**: `supplier_tracker (2).html` uses vanilla JavaScript + localStorage for persistent state per supplier.
- **Encoding issues**: Writing Polish/Portuguese characters and em-dashes in PowerShell .ps1 files via the Write tool causes corruption — needs ASCII-safe replacements.

---

3. Files and Code Sections:

**`C:\Users\aluizio\.claude\projects\c--Projetos-PROJETOS-ats-f03\memory\suppliers_status.md`**
- Completely rewritten with rich content from all 6 transcriptions.
- Now includes: per-supplier contact names, company profiles, key discussion points, open actions, Pugh rankings, new project clarifications, and a section listing Develop LLC and JR Automation as disqualified/declined.
- Added at the bottom: "Key project clarifications confirmed in supplier meetings" (HMI test not required, USB test pending, fire suppression flagged, 900 drives/day production target, Bosch Rexroth TS2 reference conveyor, load cabin 50°C fixed, first-round quotes = budget number only).

  2. Updated fire suppression entry: "flagged by multiple suppliers (Develop LLC recommended canister-style O₂-removal, like titanium machining environments; Aluizio confirmed 'we didn't think about it before but it's interesting'). Not yet formally in scope."
  3. Added global expansion note and production volume: "~900 drives/day at cruising speed (year 5)" from Bosch meeting.

**`C:\Users\aluizio\.claude\projects\c--Projetos-PROJETOS-ats-f03\memory\reference_orion_folder.md`**

**`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\supplier_tracker (2).html`**

  - `todos`: replaced with single completed item `{t:"[DONE] Sent polite disqualification email — Brazil commissioning/support is mandatory and Develop cannot provide it", d:true}`
  - `context`: replaced with 5 items documenting Matt's email text, the disqualification decision, the technical merits, and a note to keep Develop in mind for future North America expansions.

**`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\read_suppliers.ps1`** (temp file, should be deleted)

**`C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\update_suppliers.ps1`** (FAILED — encoding issue)
- Designed to completely rewrite SUPPLIERS.xlsx with 9 columns: Region, Supplier, Contact Name(s), Email(s), Outreach Status, Meeting Date, Pugh Rank, Quote/Next Step, Notes.
- Color scheme: dark navy header, blue region labels, green rows for MET, red for DECLINED/DISQUALIFIED, amber for WAITING, grey for EMAIL_SENT.

- **FAILED due to encoding**: em dashes (—), Portuguese characters (ã, ç, ô), and & in strings caused PowerShell parser errors when executed.

---

4. Errors and Fixes:

**PowerShell heredoc/inline approach failure:**
- Initial attempt using bash heredoc `<< 'PSEOF'` failed because the shell (Git Bash on Windows) doesn't properly pass heredoc to `powershell -File -`.
- Fix: Write the script to a .ps1 file first, then run with `powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "path.ps1"`.

**PowerShell variable parsing in bash command:**
- First attempt at `Bash` tool call used `$path`, `$excel`, etc. inside the command string — these were expanded by bash before PowerShell saw them, causing empty variables.
- Fix: Used the Write tool to create a .ps1 file, then invoked it separately.

**Character encoding error in update_suppliers.ps1 (CURRENT BLOCKER):**
- Error output: `Token 'nA?o' inesperado` and `Token 'verificando.' inesperado` — caused by em dashes (—) and accented characters (ã, ç) being corrupted when written via the Write tool to the .ps1 file.

- Fix needed: Replace all em dashes with hyphens or `+`, replace accented Portuguese characters with ASCII equivalents, and escape & as `"&"` or avoid it in string literals.

---

- **Meeting transcription pipeline**: docx2txt had already been run in the prior session, creating .txt files alongside the source DOCX files. Reading was straightforward with the Read tool.
- **Develop LLC disqualification**: Confirmed by Matt's email matching exactly what he said in the meeting. Disqualification email drafted in a direct, professional English tone matching Aluizio's style (no travessões, no excessive formality), leaving door open for future NA expansion.
- **HTML tracker update**: The tracker stores all runtime state (checkbox status, notes, next step text) in localStorage — the SUPPLIERS array in the JS is the "template." Updating the template data for Develop LLC was sufficient since no localStorage state existed for that entry yet.
- **XLSX encoding issue**: The PowerShell script needs to avoid special characters. The solution is to rewrite it using only ASCII characters — replace em dashes with " - ", replace ã→a, ç→c, ô→o etc., and wrap & as `"&"` or remove it.

---

6. All User Messages:

- "keep doing what you were doing" — after context compaction resumed, instructing continuation of reading MEETING TRANSCRIPTIONS
- [Screenshot image of email from Matt Moseman, Develop LLC]: Email text: "Hello Aluizio, Thank you very much for sending this over. This project aligns really well with what DEVELOP builds for machines. My biggest concern is that this needs to be delivered to Brazil. How do we want to handle that process since DEVELOP does not install machines in Brazil? Do you have a US facility we could ship to can perform SAT? We need to verify this process before quoting as it may change how we can support this project and or the associated costs to support it. Please let me know how you would like to proceed."
- "Email from develop llc, maybe we will need to stop the quotation with him"

- "please update the spreadsheet i have with suppliers contacts with all the information you have, also, format to look better"

---

7. Pending Tasks:

- **Fix and re-run update_suppliers.ps1**: The script failed due to encoding issues (em dashes, Portuguese accented characters, & symbol). Must rewrite with ASCII-only characters and re-execute to update SUPPLIERS.xlsx with the new 9-column formatted structure.
- **Clean up temp files**: Delete `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\read_suppliers.ps1` and `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\update_suppliers.ps1` after the xlsx update succeeds.

---

8. Current Work:

Immediately before this summary request, the task was updating `SUPPLIERS.xlsx` at `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\SUPPLIERS.xlsx`.

The PowerShell script `C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\update_suppliers.ps1` was written and executed but FAILED with encoding errors:

```

```

Root cause: em dashes (—), Portuguese characters (ã, ç, ô), and unquoted & were written into the .ps1 file by the Write tool with encoding corruption, making the PowerShell file unparseable.

The script was designed to:

- Write 9 columns: Region / Supplier / Contact Name(s) / Email(s) / Outreach Status / Meeting Date / Pugh Rank / Quote/Next Step / Notes

The immediate next step is to fix the encoding issue in the PowerShell script and re-run it. The fix requires rewriting `update_suppliers.ps1` replacing all non-ASCII characters with ASCII equivalents:

# Update SUPPLIERS.xlsx with full contact info and formatting
$path = 'C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\SUPPLIERS.xlsx'

$excel = New-Object -ComObject Excel.Application
$excel.Visible = $false
$excel.DisplayAlerts = $false

$wb = $excel.Workbooks.Open($path)
$ws = $wb.Sheets.Item(1)

# Clear all existing content and formatting
$ws.Cells.Clear()

# ── COLORS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$cHeader      = 0x1E3A5F   # dark navy
$cHeaderTxt   = 0xFFFFFF   # white
$cRegionBg    = 0x2E6099   # medium blue
$cRegionTxt   = 0xFFFFFF
$cMet         = 0xD6EAD6   # light green  → met & active
$cMetTxt      = 0x1A4A1A
$cDeclined    = 0xF4CCCC   # light red    → declined/disqualified
$cDecTxt      = 0x7B1A1A
$cWaiting     = 0xFFF2CC   # light amber  → waiting for reply
$cWaitTxt     = 0x7D5A00
$cPending     = 0xF3F3F3   # light grey   → email sent, no reply
$cPendTxt     = 0x444444
$cAlt         = 0xFAFAFA   # row alternation (even rows)
$cBorder      = 0xBBBBBB

# ── COLUMN DEFINITIONS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A  Region
# B  Supplier
# C  Contact Name(s)
# D  Email(s)
# E  Outreach Status
# F  Meeting Date
# G  Pugh Rank
# H  Quote Status / Next Step
# I  Notes

# ── HEADER ROW ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$headers = @("Region","Supplier","Contact Name(s)","Email(s)","Outreach Status","Meeting Date","Pugh Rank","Quote / Next Step","Notes")
for ($c = 1; $c -le $headers.Count; $c++) {
    $cell = $ws.Cells.Item(1, $c)
    $cell.Value2 = $headers[$c-1]
    $cell.Font.Bold = $true
    $cell.Font.Size = 11
    $cell.Font.Color = $cHeaderTxt
    $cell.Interior.Color = $cHeader
    $cell.HorizontalAlignment = -4108  # xlCenter
    $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4108
    $cell.WrapText = $true
}
$ws.Rows.Item(1).RowHeight = 30

# ── DATA ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Format: Region, Name, Contacts, Emails, Status, MeetingDate, PughRank, NextStep, Notes
# Status codes: MET, DECLINED, DISQUALIFIED, WAITING, EMAIL_SENT
$data = @(
  # ── BRASIL ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("BRASIL","Bosch (Campinas/Curitiba)","Marcelo Laranjeira (Comercial)`nVictor Souza (Teste Elétrico)","Marcelo.Laranjeira@br.bosch.com","MET","17/06/2026","3º (datum)","Aguardando docs WEG: prescrições de teste + tabela de dimensões + desenhos de terminais","Melhor suporte local; >60 anos; contrato exclusivo WEG Automação (>1M prod/ano). Plataforma supervisório própria. 24/7 call center."),
  @("","Autec","","projetos@autecjoi.com.br","MET","15/06/2026","5º (-11)","Aguardando cotação","Empresa local em Jaraguá do Sul. Vantagem geográfica enorme."),
  @("","Mondragon Assembly","f.dasilveira@mondragon-assembly.com","","MET","16/06/2026","-","Aguardando cotação","Linha de montagem e teste para eletrônicos. Operação local no Brasil."),
  @("","Aumaq","","vendas@aumaqrs.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","SPI","","info@integradora.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  @("","MCK Automação","","vendas@mckautomacao.com.br","EMAIL ENVIADO","-","-","Aguardando retorno","Email enviado."),
  # ── EUROPA ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  @("EUROPA","MASMEC","Stefano Piccinno (Sales Engineer)`nLuigi Di Leo","info@masmec.com","MET","16/06/2026","5º (-11)","Aguardando cotação. WEG deve: prescrição por módulo + confirmar PLC WEG (não Siemens)","Bari, Itália. Referência direta em teste de inversores automotivos (hipot, funcional, carga). Conveyor Bosch Rexroth TS2. # ── WRITE DATA ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$row = 2
$lastRegion = ""
foreach ($entry in $data) {
    for ($c = 1; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $cell = $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c)
        $cell.Value2 = $entry[$c-1]
        $cell.WrapText = $true
        $cell.VerticalAlignment = -4160  # xlTop
        $cell.Font.Size = 10
    }

    # Determine row color by status
    $status = $entry[4]
    $rowColor = $cPending
    $txtColor = $cPendTxt
    if ($status -eq "MET") { $rowColor = $cMet; $txtColor = $cMetTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "DECLINOU" -or $status -eq "DESCLASSIFICADO") { $rowColor = $cDeclined; $txtColor = $cDecTxt }
    elseif ($status -eq "WAITING") { $rowColor = $cWaiting; $txtColor = $cWaitTxt }

    # Color region column if new region
    $regionCell = $ws.Cells.Item($row, 1)
    if ($entry[0] -ne "") {
        $regionCell.Interior.Color = $cRegionBg
        $regionCell.Font.Color = $cRegionTxt
        $regionCell.Font.Bold = $true
        $regionCell.HorizontalAlignment = -4108
    }

    # Apply row background to cols B-I
    for ($c = 2; $c -le 9; $c++) {
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Interior.Color = $rowColor
        $ws.Cells.Item($row, $c).Font.Color = $txtColor
    }

    # Bold supplier name
    $ws.Cells.Item($row, 2).Font.Bold = $true

    $row++
}

# ── ROW HEIGHTS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Set all data rows to auto-fit height
for ($r = 2; $r -le $row - 1; $r++) {
    $ws.Rows.Item($r).RowHeight = 52
}

# ── COLUMN WIDTHS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Columns.Item(1).ColumnWidth = 10   # Region
$ws.Columns.Item(2).ColumnWidth = 24   # Supplier
$ws.Columns.Item(3).ColumnWidth = 28   # Contact Names
$ws.Columns.Item(4).ColumnWidth = 36   # Emails
$ws.Columns.Item(5).ColumnWidth = 16   # Status
$ws.Columns.Item(6).ColumnWidth = 14   # Meeting Date
$ws.Columns.Item(7).ColumnWidth = 12   # Pugh Rank
$ws.Columns.Item(8).ColumnWidth = 36   # Next Step
$ws.Columns.Item(9).ColumnWidth = 48   # Notes

# ── BORDERS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$dataRange = $ws.Range("A1:I" + ($row - 1))
$borders = $dataRange.Borders
$borders.Item(7).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeLeft
$borders.Item(8).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeTop
$borders.Item(9).LineStyle = 1   # xlEdgeBottom
$borders.Item(10).LineStyle = 1  # xlEdgeRight
$borders.Item(11).LineStyle = 1  # xlInsideVertical
$borders.Item(12).LineStyle = 1  # xlInsideHorizontal
$borders.Item(7).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(8).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(9).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(10).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(11).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(12).Color = $cBorder
$borders.Item(11).Weight = 1
$borders.Item(12).Weight = 1

# ── AUTOFILTER ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Range("A1:I1").AutoFilter() | Out-Null

# ── FREEZE TOP ROW ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.SplitRow = 1
$ws.Application.ActiveWindow.FreezePanes = $true

# ── LEGEND ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$legendRow = $row + 1
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Value2 = "LEGENDA"
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Font.Bold = $true
$ws.Cells.Item($legendRow, 1).Font.Size = 10

$legends = @(
  @(0, "MET — Reunião realizada, ativo no processo", $cMet, $cMetTxt),
  @(1, "EMAIL ENVIADO — Sem resposta ainda", $cPending, $cPendTxt),
  @(2, "DECLINOU / DESCLASSIFICADO", $cDeclined, $cDecTxt),
  @(3, "WAITING — Aguardando resposta específica", $cWaiting, $cWaitTxt)
)
foreach ($l in $legends) {
    $lRow = $legendRow + 1 + $l[0]
    $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1).Value2 = $l[1]
    $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1).Interior.Color = $l[2]
    $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1).Font.Color = $l[3]
    $ws.Cells.Item($lRow, 1).Font.Size = 9
    $ws.Range("A" + $lRow + ":C" + $lRow).Merge() | Out-Null
}

# ── SHEET NAME & TAB COLOR ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
$ws.Name = "Suppliers"
$ws.Tab.Color = $cHeader

# ── SHEET TITLE (merged A1 row above) — insert title row ───────────────────
$ws.Rows.Item(1).Insert() | Out-Null
$titleRange = $ws.Range("A1:I1")
$titleRange.Merge() | Out-Null
$titleRange.Value2 = "MODULAR ALL DRIVES — Supplier Contact Tracker   |   Aluizio Kleine · Industrial Engineering · WEG Drives & Controls   |   Updated: 18/06/2026"
$titleRange.Font.Bold = $true
$titleRange.Font.Size = 12
$titleRange.Font.Color = $cHeaderTxt
$titleRange.Interior.Color = $cHeader
$titleRange.HorizontalAlignment = -4108
$titleRange.VerticalAlignment = -4108
$ws.Rows.Item(1).RowHeight = 36

# ── SAVE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$wb.Save()
$wb.Close($false)
$excel.Quit()
[System.Runtime.Interopservices.Marshal]::ReleaseComObject($excel) | Out-Null

Write-Host "Done — SUPPLIERS.xlsx updated successfully."

$path = 'C:\Users\aluizio\Desktop\Aluizio\2026-04-ORION\SUPPLIERS\SUPPLIERS.xlsx'
$excel = New-Object -ComObject Excel.Application
$excel.Visible = $false
$excel.DisplayAlerts = $false
$wb = $excel.Workbooks.Open($path)
$ws = $wb.Sheets.Item(1)
$used = $ws.UsedRange
$rows = $used.Rows.Count
$cols = $used.Columns.Count
Write-Host "Rows: $rows, Cols: $cols"
for ($r = 1; $r -le $rows; $r++) {
  $line = ''
  for ($c = 1; $c -le $cols; $c++) {
    $v = $ws.Cells.Item($r, $c).Text
    $line += "[$v]"
  }
  Write-Host $line
}
$wb.Close($false)
$excel.Quit()
[System.Runtime.Interopservices.Marshal]::ReleaseComObject($excel) | Out-Null

name: suppliers-status
